topics.title: Advanced Solution Techniques - Revised Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Solution Techniques - Revised Simplex Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Revised Simplex Method (RSM):** An advanced, computationally efficient variant of the standard Simplex Method that, instead of operating on the **entire augmented tableau** of size $(m+1) \times (n+m+1)$, works exclusively with the **basis matrix $\mathbf{B}$** of dimension $m \times m$ and its inverse $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$. All required information (basic variables, simplex multipliers, reduced costs, entering/leaving directions) is **recomputed on demand** at every iteration using $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$.

In canonical form, an LP in **standard form** is:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximise } & Z = \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} \\
\text{subject to } & \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x} \ge \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is $(m \times n)$ with $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = m \le n$.

A partition of columns gives $\mathbf{A} = [\mathbf{B} \mid \mathbf{N}]$, where $\mathbf{B}$ is the **current basis** (a non-singular $m \times m$ sub-matrix). The basic solution is $\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Map vs. Following a GPS"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are navigating from Calicut to Kochi.
>
> - **Standard Simplex Method** = Carrying the **entire printed map of Kerala** folded in your bag. Every turn, you unfold the whole map, find your position, find the destination, and refold. Most of the map is irrelevant at any given moment — but you carry it anyway.
>
> - **Revised Simplex Method** = Using a **GPS that only tells you your next instruction** ("take the next left, merge onto NH66"). It only computes and displays **what is required at that exact step** — namely the direction vector and remaining distance.
>
> The "GPS" in RSM is the matrix $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$. It stores just enough information to compute everything else on the fly.

### 1.3 Why Was the Revised Method Invented?

| Standard Simplex | Revised Simplex |
|---|---|
| Stores full $(m+1) \times (n+m+1)$ tableau | Stores only $m \times m$ matrix $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ |
| Computes **all** reduced costs every iteration | Computes reduced costs **on demand** |
| Susceptible to **rounding errors** propagated through the entire tableau | Reduced errors because $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ is updated compactly |
| Poor performance on **large sparse** problems | Exploits sparsity — $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ multiplication with a single column is fast |
| Empirically slower for $n \gg m$ | **Industry standard** for real-world LP solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, MATLAB `linprog`) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Pearl:** When $n$ is very large compared to $m$ (e.g., problems with thousands of variables but few constraints), the Revised Simplex Method requires **far fewer arithmetic operations per iteration** and is the **preferred computational engine**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic Decomposition

Let $\mathbf{x}_B$ denote the vector of **basic variables** and $\mathbf{x}_N$ the **non-basic variables** (set to zero at any BFS). Partition the cost row similarly: $\mathbf{c}^\top = [\mathbf{c}_B^\top \mid \mathbf{c}_N^\top]$.

**At any iteration, the following four pieces of information are sufficient:**

1. **Current basic solution:**  $\quad \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$
2. **Simplex multipliers (dual variables / shadow prices):**  $\quad \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}$
3. **Reduced cost of a non-basic column $j$:**  $\quad \bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j} = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$
4. **Updated direction vector (column under the new basis):**  $\quad \mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$

### 2.2 The Revised Simplex Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Initialisation.**
Identify an initial feasible basis $\mathbf{B}$ (e.g., using Phase I, or a known BFS such as slack variables from $\le$ constraints). Compute $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Compute simplex multipliers.**
$$\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}$$
This is a row vector of length $m$. It does not change for all non-basic variables in the current iteration — compute **once**.

**Step 3 — Optimality test (compute reduced costs).**
For each non-basic column $\mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$, evaluate:
$$\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$$
- If **all** $\bar{c}_j \le 0$ (for a maximisation problem), the current BFS is **optimal**. **STOP.**
- Otherwise, select the entering column $q$ with the most positive $\bar{c}_q$ (Dantzig's rule).

**Step 4 — Compute the direction vector.**
$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$$
This is a column vector of length $m$. It tells how each basic variable will change as the entering variable $x_q$ increases from zero.

**Step 5 — Minimum ratio test (MRT).**
For each $i$ such that $d_i > 0$, compute $\dfrac{x_{B_i}}{d_i}$. Choose:
$$p = \arg\min_i \left\{\dfrac{x_{B_i}}{d_i} \,\bigg|\, d_i > 0\right\}$$
If **all** $d_i \le 0$, the LP is **unbounded**. **STOP.**
The leaving variable is $x_{B_p}$ (basic variable in row $p$).

**Step 6 — Update the basis.**
Replace the column of $\mathbf{B}$ corresponding to row $p$ with $\mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$. Equivalently, replace $x_{B_p}$ by $x_q$ in the basis. Update $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ using the **product form of the inverse** (Section 3).

**Step 7 — Return to Step 2.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols below are **canonical KTU notation** as used in standard textbooks (e.g., Taha, Hillier & Lieberman). Memorise the table — it covers roughly **80%** of the marks in any RSM question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Dimensions | Purpose |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----------|:--------|
| 1 | Basic solution | $\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | $m \times 1$ | RHS values for current basis |
| 2 | Simplex multipliers | $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}$ | $1 \times m$ | Dual prices, computed once per iteration |
| 3 | Reduced cost | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$ | scalar | Optimality test (entering column) |
| 4 | Direction vector | $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$ | $m \times 1$ | Pivot column under new basis |
| 5 | Minimum ratio | $\theta^\ast = \min_i \left\{\dfrac{x_{B_i}}{d_i} \;\big\vert\; d_i > 0\right\}$ | scalar | Determines leaving variable |
| 6 | New basic value | $x_{B_p}^{\text{new}} = \theta^\ast$ | scalar | Pivot row updated value |
| 7 | Other basic values | $x_{B_i}^{\text{new}} = x_{B_i} - \theta^\ast d_i,\ i \ne p$ | scalar | Updated non-pivot rows |
| 8 | Eta column (product form) | $\boldsymbol{\eta} = \left[\dfrac{-d_1}{d_p},\ldots,\dfrac{-d_{p-1}}{d_p},\dfrac{1}{d_p},\dfrac{-d_{p+1}}{d_p},\ldots,\dfrac{-d_m}{d_p}\right]^\top$ | $m \times 1$ | Update $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ compactly |
| 9 | Updated inverse | $\mathbf{B}_{\text{new}}^{-1} = \mathbf{E}_k \mathbf{B}_{\text{old}}^{-1}$ | $m \times m$ | Product form of inverse |
| 10 | Optimal $Z$-value | $Z^\ast = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{b}$ | scalar | Objective value at optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Simplex multipliers $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ are the **shadow prices** of resources. In production planning, $\pi_i$ tells the manager how much the objective (profit) would increase per unit increase in the $i$-th constraint (e.g., raw material availability). This is widely used in **supply chain optimisation, refinery scheduling, and airline revenue management**.

### 2.4 Optimality & Feasibility Conditions (Compact Form)

At any BFS with basis $\mathbf{B}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Feasibility:} \quad & \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} \ge \mathbf{0} \\
\textbf{Optimality (Max):} \quad & \bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j} \le 0,\ \forall j \in \mathcal{N}
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reduced-Cost Formula

Starting from the equality constraints $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ and partitioning columns:
$$\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{N}\mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{b} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{N}\mathbf{x}_N$$

Substituting into the objective:
$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{c}_N^\top \mathbf{x}_N \\
  &= \mathbf{c}_B^\top \left(\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{N}\mathbf{x}_N\right) + \mathbf{c}_N^\top \mathbf{x}_N \\
  &= \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} + \left(\mathbf{c}_N^\top - \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{N}\right)\mathbf{x}_N
\end{aligned}$$

Since at a BFS, $\mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{0}$, the value $Z = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{b}$.
If a non-basic $x_j$ were to increase by 1 unit (with all other non-basic at zero), $Z$ would change by the **coefficient** of $x_j$ in the above, namely:
$$\bar{c}_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{A}_{\cdot j} = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$$
This is the **reduced cost**. It is the **net gain per unit** of allowing $x_j$ into the basis.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Product-Form Inverse Update

Let the leaving variable be in row $p$. Pre-multiply both sides of $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b}$ by $\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}}$:
$$\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}}\mathbf{b}$$
Now perform the standard simplex pivot on column $q$ at row $p$. The new basis is $\mathbf{B}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{B}_{\text{old}} + (\mathbf{A}_{\cdot q} - \mathbf{B}_{\text{old}}\mathbf{e}_p)\, \mathbf{e}_p^\top$, where $\mathbf{e}_p$ is the $p$-th unit vector. One can show:
$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{E}_k\, \mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}}$$
where $\mathbf{E}_k$ differs from the identity matrix **only in column $p$**, with:
$$(\mathbf{E}_k)_{ip} = \begin{cases} 1/d_p & i = p \\ -d_i / d_p & i \ne p \end{cases}$$
Here $d_p$ is the pivot element (the $p$-th component of the direction vector $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why product form?** Storing every full $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ matrix from scratch at every iteration is wasteful. The product form stores only the sequence of **eta vectors** $\boldsymbol{\eta}_1, \boldsymbol{\eta}_2, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\eta}_k$ and reconstructs the current inverse as $\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \mathbf{E}_k \mathbf{E}_{k-1} \cdots \mathbf{E}_1 \mathbf{B}^{-1}_0$. For sparse $\mathbf{B}_0$ (often $\mathbf{I}$), this is extremely efficient.

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem.** Solve using the Revised Simplex Method:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximise } Z =\ & 5x_1 + 4x_2 \\
\text{subject to } & 6x_1 + 4x_2 \le 24 \\
& x_1 + 2x_2 \le 6 \\
& -x_1 + x_2 \le 1 \\
& x_1,\ x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 0 — Convert to standard form by adding slack variables $s_1, s_2, s_3$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
6x_1 + 4x_2 + s_1 \quad\quad\ &= 24 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 \quad\ + s_2\ \ &= 6 \\
-x_1 + x_2 \quad\quad\ + s_3 &= 1
\end{aligned}$$
Initial basis: $\mathbf{B} = \mathbf{I}_{3 \times 3}$, hence $\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$. Initial basic solution: $\mathbf{x}_B = (s_1, s_2, s_3)^\top = (24, 6, 1)^\top$. $\mathbf{c}_B = (0, 0, 0)^\top$.

Order of columns: $\mathbf{A} = [\,\mathbf{A}_1\ \mathbf{A}_2\ \mathbf{A}_3\ \mathbf{A}_4\ \mathbf{A}_5\,]$ where columns 1,2 are $x_1, x_2$ and columns 3,4,5 are $s_1, s_2, s_3$.
$$\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}6\\1\\-1\end{pmatrix},\quad \mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}4\\2\\1\end{pmatrix},\quad \mathbf{A}_3 = \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{pmatrix},\quad \mathbf{A}_4 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\\0\end{pmatrix},\quad \mathbf{A}_5 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

---

#### Iteration 0

**Compute multipliers:** $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1} = (0,0,0)^\top$.

**Reduced costs (only non-basic matter, i.e., $x_1, x_2$):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{c}_1 &= c_1 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_1 = 5 - 0 = 5 \\
\bar{c}_2 &= c_2 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_2 = 4 - 0 = 4
\end{aligned}$$

Both positive → not optimal. **Entering column** = arg max = $x_1$ (since $\bar{c}_1 = 5 > 4 = \bar{c}_2$). Set $q = 1$.

**Direction vector:**
$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{A}_1 = \mathbf{I}\mathbf{A}_1 = (6,\, 1,\, -1)^\top$$

**Minimum Ratio Test** (only $d_i > 0$ eligible):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:} &\ 24/6 = 4 \\
\text{Row 2:} &\ 6/1 = 6 \\
\text{Row 3:} &\ d_3 = -1 \le 0 \rightarrow \text{skip}
\end{aligned}$$
Minimum ratio = $4$ at row $p = 1$. **Leaving variable** = $s_1$ (row 1). Pivot element $d_p = 6$.

**Eta vector** (column 1 of $\mathbf{E}_1$, since $s_1$ is in row 1):
$$\boldsymbol{\eta}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{6},\ \frac{-1}{6},\ \frac{1}{6}\right)^\top = \left(0.1667,\ -0.1667,\ 0.1667\right)^\top$$

**New basic values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1^{\text{new}} &= \theta^\ast = 4 \\
s_2^{\text{new}} &= 6 - 4\cdot 1 = 2 \\
s_3^{\text{new}} &= 1 - 4\cdot(-1) = 5
\end{aligned}$$

**Update basis:** $s_1$ leaves, $x_1$ enters.
$$\mathbf{B}_{\text{new}}^{-1} = \mathbf{E}_1\,\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}} = \begin{pmatrix}1/6 & 0 & 0\\-1/6 & 1 & 0\\1/6 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1/6 & 0 & 0\\-1/6 & 1 & 0\\1/6 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$$

New basis order: $\{x_1, s_2, s_3\}$. So $\mathbf{c}_B^{\text{new}} = (5,\, 0,\, 0)^\top$.

---

#### Iteration 1

**Compute multipliers:**
$$\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1} = (5, 0, 0) \begin{pmatrix}1/6 & 0 & 0\\-1/6 & 1 & 0\\1/6 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix} = \left(\tfrac{5}{6},\ 0,\ 0\right)$$

**Reduced costs** (for non-basic $x_2$ and $s_1$):

> Recall non-basic variables now: $x_2, s_1$. The original $s_1$ column is $\mathbf{A}_3 = (1,0,0)^\top$.
$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{c}_2 &= c_2 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_2 = 4 - \left(\tfrac{5}{6}\cdot 4 + 0\cdot 2 + 0\cdot 1\right) = 4 - \tfrac{20}{6} = \tfrac{24-20}{6} = \tfrac{4}{6} = \tfrac{2}{3} > 0 \\
\bar{c}_{s_1} &= c_{s_1} - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_3 = 0 - \tfrac{5}{6} = -\tfrac{5}{6} < 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Entering column** = $x_2$ (positive reduced cost). $q = 2$.

**Direction vector:**
$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{A}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}1/6 & 0 & 0\\-1/6 & 1 & 0\\1/6 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}4\\2\\1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}4/6\\-4/6+2\\4/6+1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2/3\\4/3\\5/3\end{pmatrix}$$

**Minimum Ratio Test:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1 (}x_1\text{):} &\ \dfrac{4}{2/3} = 6 \\
\text{Row 2 (}s_2\text{):} &\ \dfrac{2}{4/3} = \tfrac{3}{2} \\
\text{Row 3 (}s_3\text{):} &\ \dfrac{5}{5/3} = 3
\end{aligned}$$
Minimum = $\tfrac{3}{2}$ at row $p = 2$. **Leaving variable** = $s_2$. Pivot element $d_p = \tfrac{4}{3}$.

**Eta vector** (column 2 of $\mathbf{E}_2$):
$$\boldsymbol{\eta}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}-d_1/d_p \\ 1/d_p \\ -d_3/d_p\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-(2/3)/(4/3)\\ (4/3)^{-1}\\ -(5/3)/(4/3)\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-1/2\\ 3/4\\ -5/4\end{pmatrix}$$

So $\mathbf{E}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}1 & -1/2 & 0\\ 0 & 3/4 & 0\\ 0 & -5/4 & 1\end{pmatrix}$.

**New basic values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
x_2^{\text{new}} &= \theta^\ast = \tfrac{3}{2} \\
x_1^{\text{new}} &= 4 - \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{2}{3} = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
s_3^{\text{new}} &= 5 - \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{5}{3} = 5 - \tfrac{5}{2} = \tfrac{5}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Update inverse:**
$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{E}_2 \mathbf{E}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}1 & -1/2 & 0\\ 0 & 3/4 & 0\\ 0 & -5/4 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1/6 & 0 & 0\\-1/6 & 1 & 0\\1/6 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$$

Row-wise computation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Row 1:} &\ \left(\tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{12},\ -\tfrac{1}{2},\ 0\right) = \left(\tfrac{1}{4},\ -\tfrac{1}{2},\ 0\right) \\
\text{Row 2:} &\ \left(-\tfrac{1}{8},\ \tfrac{3}{4},\ 0\right) \\
\text{Row 3:} &\ \left(\tfrac{1}{6} - \tfrac{5}{24},\ -\tfrac{5}{4},\ 1\right) = \left(-\tfrac{1}{24},\ -\tfrac{5}{4},\ 1\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{new}} = \begin{pmatrix}1/4 & -1/2 & 0\\ -1/8 & 3/4 & 0\\ -1/24 & -5/4 & 1\end{pmatrix}$$

New basis order: $\{x_1, x_2, s_3\}$. $\mathbf{c}_B = (5, 4, 0)^\top$.

---

#### Iteration 2

**Compute multipliers:**
$$\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = (5, 4, 0)\begin{pmatrix}1/4 & -1/2 & 0\\ -1/8 & 3/4 & 0\\ -1/24 & -5/4 & 1\end{pmatrix}$$

Compute component-wise:
$$\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= 5\cdot\tfrac{1}{4} + 4\cdot\left(-\tfrac{1}{8}\right) + 0 = \tfrac{5}{4} - \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{3}{4} \\
\pi_2 &= 5\cdot(-\tfrac{1}{2}) + 4\cdot\tfrac{3}{4} + 0 = -\tfrac{5}{2} + 3 = \tfrac{1}{2} \\
\pi_3 &= 0\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 0 + 0\cdot 1 = 0
\end{aligned}$$
Hence $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \left(\tfrac{3}{4},\ \tfrac{1}{2},\ 0\right)$.

**Reduced costs** (non-basic now: $s_1, s_2$):
$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{c}_{s_1} &= 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_3 = 0 - \tfrac{3}{4}\cdot 1 = -\tfrac{3}{4} < 0 \\
\bar{c}_{s_2} &= 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_4 = 0 - \left(\tfrac{3}{4}\cdot 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 1 + 0\right) = -\tfrac{1}{2} < 0
\end{aligned}$$

**All reduced costs $\le 0$. Optimality reached.**

---

### 3.4 Final Solution

$$\boxed{\,x_1^\ast = 3,\quad x_2^\ast = \tfrac{3}{2},\quad Z^\ast = 5(3) + 4(\tfrac{3}{2}) = 15 + 6 = 21\,}$$

Verification by plugging back:
- Constraint 1: $6(3) + 4(\tfrac{3}{2}) = 18 + 6 = 24 \le 24$ ✓ (tight)
- Constraint 2: $3 + 2(\tfrac{3}{2}) = 3 + 3 = 6 \le 6$ ✓ (tight)
- Constraint 3: $-3 + \tfrac{3}{2} = -\tfrac{3}{2} \le 1$ ✓ (slack)

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Step Matters

| Step | What it computes | Why it matters |
|:----:|:-----------------|:---------------|
| Compute $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top$ | Simplex multipliers — done **once per iteration** | Saves $n$ inner products instead of $n$ during reduced-cost pass |
| Reduced costs | Only over non-basic columns | If most variables are non-basic in large problems, this is a **massive saving** |
| Direction vector $\mathbf{d}$ | Solves $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$ | Done by forward substitution; exploits sparsity of $\mathbf{B}$ |
| MRT | Determines feasibility preservation | Standard simplex logic, just embedded in compact form |
| Eta vector update | Compact, single-column update to $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ | Avoids recomputing the full inverse each iteration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Revised Simplex Algorithm Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Initial basis B, B-inverse, x_B]) --> B[Compute pi transpose = c_B transpose B-inverse]
    B --> C[For each non-basic column j, compute reduced cost c_bar_j = c_j - pi transpose A_j]
    C --> D{All c_bar_j <= 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E([STOP: Optimal BFS reached. Output x_B and Z])
    D -- No --> F[Select entering column q = arg max c_bar_j]
    F --> G[Compute direction vector d = B-inverse A_q]
    G --> H{All d_i <= 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I([STOP: LP is Unbounded])
    H -- No --> J[Min Ratio Test: p = argmin x_Bi over d_i, with d_i > 0]
    J --> K[Identify leaving variable x_Bp, pivot d_p]
    K --> L[Construct eta column and update B-inverse via product form]
    L --> M[Update x_B using new basis values]
    M --> N[Replace x_Bp with x_q in basis vector c_B]
    N --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Computational Flow Per Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Setup["Inputs (carried from previous iteration)"]
        Binv["B-inverse matrix"]
        cB["c_B vector"]
        xB["x_B basic values"]
    end
    subgraph Core["Per-iteration computations"]
        M1["Step A: pi = c_B transpose B-inverse"]
        M2["Step B: reduced costs for non-basic j"]
        M3["Step C: direction vector d for chosen q"]
        M4["Step D: minimum ratio test for leaving row p"]
    end
    subgraph Update["Basis update"]
        U1["Build Eta column from d and d_p"]
        U2["Update B-inverse_new = Eta times B-inverse_old"]
        U3["Update x_B_new and c_B_new"]
    end
    Outputs["Outputs: x_B, B-inverse, c_B for next iteration"]
    Binv --> M1
    cB --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    xB --> M4
    M4 --> U1
    M4 --> U2
    M4 --> U3
    Binv --> U2
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> Outputs
    U3 --> Outputs
```

### 4.3 Data-Flow Schematic — Information Reuse Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OncePerIter["Computed Once per Iteration"]
        P["pi transpose vector length m"]
    end
    subgraph MultiplePerIter["Computed for each non-basic j"]
        R["Reduced cost c_bar_j scalar"]
        D["Direction vector d only for chosen q"]
    end
    subgraph Always["Always available"]
        Binv["B-inverse m x m"]
    end
    Binv --> P
    P --> R
    Binv --> D
    R --> Decision{"Pick entering column q"}
    Decision --> D
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Set B]**

**Q1.** *"What is the Revised Simplex Method? State two advantages over the standard Simplex Method."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Revised Simplex Method is a **computationally efficient version of the Simplex Method** that, instead of working with the entire $(m+1)\times(n+m+1)$ tableau, stores and updates only the **basis matrix $\mathbf{B}$ and its inverse $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$** of size $m \times m$. All other quantities — basic solution, simplex multipliers, reduced costs, and direction vectors — are **computed on demand** at each iteration using $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$.

**Two advantages (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Computational efficiency in storage and time:** For problems with $n \gg m$, the Revised Simplex performs far fewer arithmetic operations per iteration. Reduced costs for non-basic columns are computed only when needed, not for all $n$ columns at once.
2. **Better numerical accuracy and exploitation of sparsity:** Updating $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ through the product form (single-column eta updates) reduces the accumulation of rounding errors compared to repeatedly operating on the full tableau. Sparse $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ multiplications with a single column $\mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$ are very fast in modern LP solvers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2.** *"Define simplex multipliers and reduced costs. How are they used in the Revised Simplex Method?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Simplex multipliers (1 mark):** The row vector $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}$ of dimension $1 \times m$, computed once per iteration from the current basis costs and inverse. They represent the **dual (shadow) prices** of the constraints.

**Reduced cost (1 mark):** For any non-basic variable $x_j$, the scalar
$$\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$$
which measures the **net improvement in $Z$ per unit increase** of $x_j$ above its current value of zero.

**Usage (1 mark):** In the Revised Simplex Method, $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top$ is computed once per iteration. Then $\bar{c}_j$ is evaluated for each non-basic $j$. If all $\bar{c}_j \le 0$ (maximisation), the BFS is optimal. Otherwise, the variable with the largest positive $\bar{c}_j$ enters the basis.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Consider the LP:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximise } Z =\ & 3x_1 + 5x_2 \\
\text{subject to } & 3x_1 + 2x_2 \le 18 \\
& x_1 \le 4 \\
& 2x_2 \le 12 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$
Solve the problem using the **Revised Simplex Method**. Show clearly the computation of simplex multipliers, reduced costs, direction vectors, minimum ratio test, and the product-form inverse update at every iteration.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **product form of the inverse** used in the Revised Simplex Method. How does it differ from the explicit computation of the full inverse at each iteration? Use the eta vector formulation:
$$\boldsymbol{\eta} = \left[\frac{-d_1}{d_p},\ldots,\frac{-d_{p-1}}{d_p},\frac{1}{d_p},\frac{-d_{p+1}}{d_p},\ldots,\frac{-d_m}{d_p}\right]^\top$$
and show how $\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{E}_k\,\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Consider:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximise } Z =\ & 4x_1 + 3x_2 \\
\text{subject to } & 2x_1 + x_2 \le 10 \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \le 12 \\
& x_2 \le 4 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$
Apply the **Revised Simplex Method** to obtain the optimal solution. Tabulate each iteration with: current basis, $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$, simplex multipliers, reduced costs for non-basic variables, direction vector, minimum ratio test result, and new basic values.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Compare the **standard Simplex Method** and the **Revised Simplex Method** in terms of:
(i) storage requirements, (ii) computational effort per iteration, (iii) numerical accuracy, (iv) suitability for sparse large-scale problems. Mention one industrial application where the Revised Simplex Method is preferred.

---

### Model Solution — Question A, Part (a) (Worked Sketch for Self-Practice)

**Standard form** with slacks $s_1, s_2, s_3$:
$$\begin{aligned}
3x_1 + 2x_2 + s_1 &= 18 \\
x_1 + s_2 &= 4 \\
2x_2 + s_3 &= 12
\end{aligned}$$
Initial basis $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$, $\mathbf{x}_B = (18, 4, 12)^\top$.

**Iteration 0:** $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = (0,0,0)$. Reduced costs: $\bar{c}_1 = 3$, $\bar{c}_2 = 5$. Enter $x_2$. $\mathbf{d} = (2, 0, 2)^\top$. Ratios: $18/2 = 9$, row 2 skipped ($d_2=0$), $12/2 = 6$. Min ratio $=6$, leave $s_3$. Eta column $(1)$: $\boldsymbol{\eta}_1 = (-1, 0, 1/2)^\top$. New basis: $\{s_1, s_2, x_2\}$. New $\mathbf{x}_B = (6, 4, 6)^\top$. $\mathbf{B}^{-1}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & -1\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1/2\end{pmatrix}$.

**Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{c}_B = (0, 0, 5)^\top$. $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = (0, 0, 5)\mathbf{B}^{-1}_1 = (0, 0, 5/2)$. Reduced cost for $x_1$:
$$\bar{c}_1 = 3 - (0\cdot 3 + 0\cdot 1 + \tfrac{5}{2}\cdot 0) = 3 > 0$$
(For $s_3$: $\bar{c}_{s_3} = 0 - 5/2 = -5/2 < 0$.) Enter $x_1$.
$$\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}_1\mathbf{A}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & -1\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1/2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}3\\1\\0\end{pmatrix} = (3, 1, 0)^\top$$
Ratios: $6/3 = 2$, $4/1 = 4$, row 3 skipped. Min = $2$, leave $s_1$. Eta column $(1)$: $\boldsymbol{\eta}_2 = (1/3, -1/3, 0)^\top$.

New basis: $\{x_1, s_2, x_2\}$. $\mathbf{x}_B = (2, 2, 6)^\top$. $\mathbf{c}_B = (3, 0, 5)^\top$.

**Iteration 2:** $\mathbf{B}^{-1}_2 = \mathbf{E}_2 \mathbf{B}^{-1}_1$. $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = (3, 0, 5)\mathbf{B}^{-1}_2 = \ldots$ (work out: $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = (1, 0, 5/2)$). Reduced costs:
- $\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - (1\cdot 1 + 0\cdot 0 + \tfrac{5}{2}\cdot 0) = -1 < 0$
- $\bar{c}_{s_3} = 0 - (0 + 0 + \tfrac{5}{2}\cdot 1) = -\tfrac{5}{2} < 0$

**All reduced costs $\le 0$. Optimal.**

$$x_1^\ast = 2,\ x_2^\ast = 6,\ Z^\ast = 3(2) + 5(6) = 6 + 30 = \boxed{36}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute $\mathbf{c}_B$ after basis change.** Many students keep the old cost vector across iterations, leading to **completely wrong simplex multipliers** and hence wrong reduced costs. The cost of every basic variable must match the **current basis order**.
> 2. **Confusing the pivot column and pivot row.** In the Revised Simplex, the *entering* column is selected from the reduced costs (column pivot), and the *leaving* row comes from the minimum ratio test (row pivot). Students sometimes swap these, giving nonsensical $\theta$ values.
> 3. **Skipping the eta-column computation.** A frequent 2-mark loss. Even if the next basis is correct, the **explicit eta vector** must be shown to earn full marks on product-form questions.
> 4. **Sign error in reduced cost.** The formula is $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top\mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$, **not** $c_j + \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top\mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$. Both the **minus sign and the transpose** are common error points.
> 5. **Not checking the unbounded case.** If all $d_i \le 0$, students often continue the iteration mechanically. Examiners specifically test for **correct identification of unboundedness** — always state the conclusion explicitly.
> 6. **Missing the final $Z^\ast$ value.** Even if the optimal $x_1, x_2$ are correctly computed, failure to state $Z^\ast$ explicitly costs 1 mark in most valuation schemes.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The Revised Simplex Method uses only $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ (size $m \times m$), not the full tableau.
- [ ] **Three core per-iteration formulas:** (i) $\boldsymbol{\pi}^\top = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1}$, (ii) $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{A}_{\cdot j}$, (iii) $\mathbf{d} = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{A}_{\cdot q}$.
- [ ] The **entering variable** is the non-basic $x_q$ with the **most positive** $\bar{c}_j$ (Dantzig's rule).
- [ ] The **leaving variable** is the basic $x_{B_p}$ corresponding to the **minimum positive ratio** $x_{B_i}/d_i$.
- [ ] **Unboundedness:** if all $d_i \le 0$, the LP is unbounded.
- [ ] **Optimality:** if all $\bar{c}_j \le 0$ (maximisation), stop.
- [ ] **Product form of inverse:** $\mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{E}_k \mathbf{B}^{-1}_{\text{old}}$, where $\mathbf{E}_k$ is an identity matrix modified in **only one column** (the eta column).
- [ ] The eta column has $1/d_p$ in the pivot row and $-d_i/d_p$ in all other rows.
- [ ] $\mathbf{c}_B$ **must be updated** whenever the basis composition changes.
- [ ] Simplex multipliers $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ have **economic meaning** as shadow prices of resources.
- [ ] The Revised Simplex Method is preferred when $n \gg m$ (many variables, few constraints).
- [ ] At optimum, $Z^\ast = \mathbf{c}_B^\top \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{b} = \boldsymbol{\pi}^\top \mathbf{b}$.
- [ ] The algorithm **always recomputes** the basis inverse from scratch via eta products — never store every $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ explicitly across iterations.
- [ ] Industrial solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, MATLAB `linprog`) use Revised Simplex (or interior-point) as their default LP engine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dual Simplex Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Dual Simplex Method (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **Dual Simplex Method** is an iterative linear programming algorithm that operates in reverse of the standard (primal) simplex method. It begins with a basic solution that is **primal infeasible** but **dual feasible**, and proceeds through pivoting operations to restore primal feasibility while preserving dual optimality, eventually converging to the optimal solution of the LPP.

In simpler KTU textbook terms:

> The Dual Simplex Method is employed when an initial **basic feasible solution (BFS)** to the dual problem is readily available, but the primal BFS violates one or more primal constraints (i.e., has negative right-hand side values).

The method is the **dual** counterpart of the standard simplex algorithm:

| Parameter | Primal Simplex | Dual Simplex |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Primal feasible, Dual infeasible | Dual feasible, Primal infeasible |
| **Iteration Direction** | Moves along the boundary toward feasibility of dual | Moves along the boundary toward feasibility of primal |
| **Optimality Condition** | All reduced costs (c̄ⱼ − z̄ⱼ) ≥ 0 | All RHS values (X_B) ≥ 0 |
| **Use Case** | Standard maximization with ≤ constraints | Problems with ≥, = constraints or post-optimality analysis |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Tightrope Walker**
> Imagine a tightrope walker attempting to cross from one building to another.
> - In **Primal Simplex**, the walker starts on a safe platform inside the building (feasible) and walks outward to the tightrope, seeking balance (optimality).
> - In **Dual Simplex**, the walker is **already balanced on the tightrope** (dual feasible / optimally oriented) but one foot is **dangling off the platform** (primal infeasible). The walker must carefully step back onto the platform while maintaining balance.
> 
> The "stepping back" is the pivot operation — the walker picks the most unstable foot (most negative RHS = leaving variable) and steps onto the safest plank (entering variable) via minimum ratio test.

**Another simpler analogy (Budget Allocation):**
Suppose a company has a fixed budget (dual constraint) that must not be exceeded, but the current production plan overshoots some resource limit. The dual simplex method **adjusts the production variables** to bring resource usage back within budget, while ensuring the **profit per unit remains maximized**.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Form Required for Dual Simplex**
> The LPP must be in the form:
> $$\text{Maximize } Z = c^T x$$
> $$\text{Subject to } Ax = b, \quad x \geq 0$$
> 
> Where the starting basis must have **c̄ⱼ − z̄ⱼ ≤ 0 for all non-basic variables** (dual feasibility), but at least one **bᵢ < 0** (primal infeasibility).
> 
> Standard convention: **Maximization** with all **≤** constraints → ready for dual simplex directly if RHS has negatives.

## 1.4 When to Use Dual Simplex

> [!TIP]
> **Primary Applications in KTU Curriculum:**
> 1. Solving LPPs with **≥** or **=** constraints where artificial variables would otherwise be required.
> 2. **Post-optimality (Sensitivity) Analysis** when the RHS vector **b** changes.
> 3. **Re-optimization** after adding new constraints that violate feasibility.
> 4. Integer programming as a sub-problem solver in **branch-and-bound**.
> 5. **Cutting plane methods** in Gomory's algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Dual Simplex Method is rooted in the **Duality Theorem of Linear Programming**. Every LPP (called the **primal**) has a corresponding **dual** LPP. The relationship is:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Duality Principle**
> If the primal has an optimal solution, so does the dual, and the optimal objective values are **equal**: $\max Z_p = \min W_d$.

The Dual Simplex Method exploits the property that when a solution is **dual feasible**, it satisfies the optimality condition. Only primal feasibility (b ≥ 0) needs to be restored through pivot operations.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Steps (Formal)

### **Step 1: Set Up the Initial Tableau**
Convert the LPP to standard form and construct the initial simplex tableau with slack/surplus variables as the starting basis. The starting basis must be **dual feasible** (all cⱼ − zⱼ ≤ 0).

### **Step 2: Optimality Test (Primal Feasibility Check)**
Examine the **right-hand side (RHS)** values bᵢ of all basic variable equations:
- If **all bᵢ ≥ 0** → The solution is **optimal**. Stop.
- If **any bᵢ < 0** → Proceed to Step 3.

### **Step 3: Select the Leaving Variable**
Choose the row with the **most negative RHS value**:
$$\text{Leaving row } r = \arg\min_i \{b_i \mid b_i < 0\}$$

The corresponding basic variable $x_{B_r}$ **leaves the basis**. If ties exist, choose arbitrarily.

### **Step 4: Select the Entering Variable (Minimum Ratio Test — Dual Version)**
For the leaving row $r$, examine the coefficients $a_{rj}$ of non-basic variables in the tableau. Only consider **negative** coefficients $a_{rj} < 0$. Compute the ratio:
$$\text{Ratio}_j = \frac{c_j - z_j}{-a_{rj}} = \frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|}$$

Select:
$$\text{Entering column } s = \arg\min_{j: a_{rj} < 0} \left\{ \frac{c_j - z_j}{-a_{rj}} \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Only columns with **negative pivot-row coefficients** ($a_{rj} < 0$) are considered. If no such column exists, the LPP has **no feasible solution** (dual infeasibility → primal infeasibility).

### **Step 5: Pivot Operation**
Perform the standard Gauss-Jordan elimination using the pivot element $a_{rs}$:
- New pivot row: divide row $r$ by $a_{rs}$
- Eliminate the pivot column from all other rows

### **Step 6: Update the Basis**
Replace $x_{B_r}$ with $x_s$ in the basis. Go to **Step 2**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $b_i$ | RHS of basic variable equation | Must be $\geq 0$ for optimality |
| $c_j - z_j$ | Reduced cost (net evaluation row) | Must be $\leq 0$ (maximization) for dual feasibility |
| Leaving row index $r$ | Most negative RHS | $r = \min\{i : b_i < 0\}$ |
| Entering column index $s$ | Min ratio | $s = \min\left\{\frac{c_j - z_j}{-a_{rj}} : a_{rj} < 0\right\}$ |
| Pivot element $a_{rs}$ | Intersection of leaving row and entering column | Used for Gauss-Jordan elimination |
| $Z^*$ | Optimal objective value | Read from bottom-right of tableau |
| Primal feasible | All $b_i \geq 0$ | Sufficient for termination in Dual Simplex |
| Dual feasible | All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ (max) | Required starting condition |
| Unbounded (dual) | No negative $a_{rj}$ in leaving row | Implies primal infeasibility |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Applications of Dual Simplex:**
> - **Supply Chain Optimization**: When warehouse capacity changes (RHS perturbation), dual simplex re-optimizes without rebuilding the entire solution.
> - **Power Systems**: Economic dispatch with binding constraints.
> - **Network Flow Problems**: Sensitivity to capacity changes.
> - **Production Planning**: When new regulations impose additional ≤ constraints.
> - **Telecommunications**: Bandwidth allocation problems.

## 2.5 Relationship with Primal Simplex (Theoretical Insight)

The Dual Simplex Method applied to a primal LPP is **mathematically equivalent** to applying the Primal Simplex Method to the dual LPP. This duality is the foundation of computational efficiency in linear programming.

If we have:
- **Primal**: Max $c^T x$ subject to $Ax \leq b$, $x \geq 0$
- **Dual**: Min $b^T w$ subject to $A^T w \geq c$, $w \geq 0$

A BFS of the primal that violates some constraints but satisfies all dual constraints will be an optimal solution to the dual when pivoting restores primal feasibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Dual Simplex Ratio Test

Let the current tableau have basic variables $x_B = B^{-1} b$ and the reduced cost row $c_N - c_B^T B^{-1} N$. The optimality condition in maximization requires:
$$c_N^T - c_B^T B^{-1} N \leq 0 \quad \text{(dual feasibility)}$$

Suppose one basic variable $x_{B_r} < 0$ (primal infeasibility). We want to bring this variable to zero by introducing some non-basic variable $x_s$.

The pivot row $r$ gives:
$$x_{B_r} + \sum_{j \in N} a_{rj} x_j = b_r$$

To bring $x_{B_r} = 0$ while maintaining $x_{B_r} \geq 0$ for all OTHER basic variables, we need to express other basic variables in terms of $x_s$:
$$x_{B_i} = b_i - a_{is} x_s$$

For all other basic variables to remain non-negative:
$$b_i - a_{is} x_s \geq 0 \implies x_s \leq \frac{b_i}{a_{is}} \quad \text{if } a_{is} > 0$$
$$b_i - a_{is} x_s \geq 0 \implies x_s \geq \frac{b_i}{a_{is}} \quad \text{if } a_{is} < 0$$

Since $x_s \geq 0$, we need:
$$x_s \leq \min_{i \neq r, a_{is} > 0} \frac{b_i}{a_{is}}$$

For **dual feasibility** to be preserved (i.e., the reduced cost of the new basic variable $x_s$ becomes 0 and other non-basic variables remain non-positive), we need:
$$\frac{c_s - z_s}{-a_{rs}} = \min_{j: a_{rj} < 0} \frac{c_j - z_j}{-a_{rj}}$$

This gives the **Dual Simplex Minimum Ratio Test**.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style)

### **Problem:**
Solve the following LPP using the Dual Simplex Method:

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 3x_1 + 2x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$x_1 + x_2 \geq 4$$
$$2x_1 + x_2 \geq 6$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

### **Step 1: Convert to Standard Form**

Subtract surplus variables $s_1, s_2$ and add artificial variables $a_1, a_2$... 

**However**, we observe that since we have $\geq$ constraints, the Big-M method would require artificial variables. But for **Dual Simplex**, we can avoid this if we cleverly reformulate.

**Alternative Conversion for Dual Simplex:**
We multiply constraints by $-1$ to flip inequality direction:
$$-x_1 - x_2 \leq -4$$
$$-2x_1 - x_2 \leq -6$$

Now add slack variables $s_1, s_2$:
$$-x_1 - x_2 + s_1 = -4$$
$$-2x_1 - x_2 + s_2 = -6$$

The starting basis is $s_1 = -4$, $s_2 = -6$ (both negative — primal infeasible).

The objective function row: $Z - 3x_1 - 2x_2 = 0$

Check dual feasibility: $c_j - z_j$ for $x_1$ is $-3$ and for $x_2$ is $-2$ (both $\leq 0$ ✓).

### **Step 2: Initial Tableau**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-4$ |
| $s_2$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-6$ |
| $Z$-row | $-3$ | $-2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

### **Step 3: Iteration 1**

**Leaving Variable:** Most negative RHS is $-6$ in row 2. So $s_2$ leaves the basis.

**Entering Variable:** Consider row 2 coefficients. Only $x_1$ and $x_2$ have negative coefficients ($-2$ and $-1$). Compute ratios:

$$\text{Ratio for } x_1 = \frac{c_1 - z_1}{-a_{21}} = \frac{-3}{-(-2)} = \frac{-3}{2} = -1.5$$

$$\text{Ratio for } x_2 = \frac{c_2 - z_2}{-a_{22}} = \frac{-2}{-(-1)} = \frac{-2}{1} = -2$$

Wait — for dual simplex, we need **non-negative ratios** when $a_{rj} < 0$. Let's recalculate carefully:

$$\text{Ratio for } x_1 = \frac{-3}{2} = -1.5 \quad (\text{since } a_{21} = -2 < 0)$$
$$\text{Ratio for } x_2 = \frac{-2}{1} = -2 \quad (\text{since } a_{22} = -1 < 0)$$

The minimum ratio is $\min(-1.5, -2) = -2$, so **$x_2$ enters the basis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Sign Convention:** Some textbooks use $\frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|}$ giving positive ratios. With this convention, $x_1$ has ratio $1.5$ and $x_2$ has ratio $2$. The minimum is $1.5$, so $x_1$ enters. We'll use this standard convention going forward for clarity.

Using the convention $\min \frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|}$:

$$\frac{|{-3}|}{|-2|} = 1.5, \quad \frac{|{-2}|}{|-1|} = 2$$

Minimum is $1.5$, so **$x_1$ enters**.

**Pivot Element:** $a_{21} = -2$.

### **Step 4: Pivot on $a_{21} = -2$**

**New Row 2** = Old Row 2 $\div (-2)$:
$$x_2 + (-2/-2) = -2/-2 \implies x_2 \text{ row} : [1, 0.5, 0, -0.5, 3]$$

Wait, let me redo this carefully:

Old Row 2: $[-2, -1, 0, 1, -6]$

Divide by $-2$:
$$x_1 \text{ coefficient: } -2 / -2 = 1$$
$$x_2 \text{ coefficient: } -1 / -2 = 0.5$$
$$s_1 \text{ coefficient: } 0 / -2 = 0$$
$$s_2 \text{ coefficient: } 1 / -2 = -0.5$$
$$\text{RHS: } -6 / -2 = 3$$

**New Row 2:** $[1, 0.5, 0, -0.5, 3]$

**New Row 1** = Old Row 1 + New Row 2 (to eliminate $x_1$ coefficient in row 1):
Old Row 1: $[-1, -1, 1, 0, -4]$
Add New Row 2: $[1, 0.5, 0, -0.5, 3]$

New Row 1: $[0, -0.5, 1, -0.5, -1]$

**New Z-row** = Old Z-row + $3 \times$ New Row 2:
Old Z-row: $[-3, -2, 0, 0, 0]$
$3 \times$ New Row 2: $[3, 1.5, 0, -1.5, 9]$

New Z-row: $[0, -0.5, 0, -1.5, 9]$

### **Step 5: Tableau After Iteration 1**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $1$ | $-0.5$ | $-1$ |
| $x_1$ | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $3$ |
| $Z$-row | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $0$ | $-1.5$ | $9$ |

### **Step 6: Iteration 2**

**Leaving Variable:** Most negative RHS is $-1$ in row 1. So $s_1$ leaves.

**Entering Variable:** Consider row 1. Negative coefficients: $x_2 = -0.5$, $s_2 = -0.5$.

Compute ratios:
$$\frac{|c_2 - z_2|}{|a_{12}|} = \frac{|-0.5|}{|-0.5|} = 1$$
$$\frac{|c_{s_2} - z_{s_2}|}{|a_{1,s_2}|} = \frac{|-1.5|}{|-0.5|} = 3$$

Minimum is $1$, so **$x_2$ enters**.

**Pivot Element:** $a_{12} = -0.5$.

### **Step 7: Pivot on $a_{12} = -0.5$**

**New Row 1** = Old Row 1 $\div (-0.5)$:
$$x_2 \text{ coefficient: } -0.5 / -0.5 = 1$$
$$x_1 \text{ coefficient: } 0 / -0.5 = 0$$
$$s_1 \text{ coefficient: } 1 / -0.5 = -2$$
$$s_2 \text{ coefficient: } -0.5 / -0.5 = 1$$
$$\text{RHS: } -1 / -0.5 = 2$$

New Row 1: $[0, 1, -2, 1, 2]$

**New Row 2** = Old Row 2 $- 0.5 \times$ New Row 1:
Old Row 2: $[1, 0.5, 0, -0.5, 3]$
$-0.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, -0.5, 1, -0.5, -1]$

New Row 2: $[1, 0, 1, -1, 2]$

**New Z-row** = Old Z-row + $0.5 \times$ New Row 1:
Old Z-row: $[0, -0.5, 0, -1.5, 9]$
$0.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, 0.5, -1, 0.5, 1]$

New Z-row: $[0, 0, -1, -1, 10]$

### **Step 8: Final Tableau**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_2$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-2$ | $1$ | $2$ |
| $x_1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-1$ | $2$ |
| $Z$-row | $0$ | $0$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $10$ |

### **Step 9: Optimality Check**

All RHS values are $\geq 0$ ($x_1 = 2 \geq 0$, $x_2 = 2 \geq 0$). All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$. **Solution is optimal!**

### **Final Answer**

$$x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 2, \quad Z_{\max} = 10$$

**Verification:** Substitute back into constraints:
- $x_1 + x_2 = 4 \geq 4$ ✓ (binding)
- $2x_1 + x_2 = 6 \geq 6$ ✓ (binding)
- $Z = 3(2) + 2(2) = 10$ ✓

## 3.3 Special Case: No Negative Coefficients in Leaving Row

If the leaving row has no negative coefficients, the LPP has **no feasible solution**. This is detected as:
$$\text{No } j \text{ exists with } a_{rj} < 0 \implies \text{Primal infeasibility}$$

This corresponds to **dual unboundedness**, which by the duality theorem implies **primal infeasibility**.

## 3.4 Comparison Table: Primal vs. Dual Simplex Rules

| Step | Primal Simplex | Dual Simplex |
|---|---|---|
| **Selection Criterion** | Most positive $c_j - z_j$ | Most negative $b_i$ |
| **Ratio Test** | $\min \frac{c_j - z_j}{a_{ij}}$ for $a_{ij} > 0$ | $\min \frac{c_j - z_j}{-a_{rj}}$ for $a_{rj} < 0$ |
| **Termination** | All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ | All $b_i \geq 0$ |
| **Stop Condition** | All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ | All $b_i \geq 0$ |
| **Stopping Condition** | Optimal reached | Optimal reached |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Dual Simplex Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Construct Initial Tableau] --> B{All RHS Values b_i Greater Than or Equal to 0?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Solution is Optimal. Stop.]
    B -- No --> D[Identify Most Negative RHS: b_r = min b_i where b_i less than 0]
    D --> E[Leaving Variable: x_Br leaves the basis]
    E --> F{Any Negative Coefficient a_rj in Row r?}
    F -- No --> G[LPP has NO FEASIBLE SOLUTION. Stop.]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Ratios: c_j minus z_j divided by minus a_rj for all a_rj less than 0]
    H --> I[Select Entering Column s: minimum ratio]
    I --> J[Entering Variable: x_s enters the basis]
    J --> K[Perform Gauss-Jordan Pivot on element a_rs]
    K --> L[Update Tableau and Basis]
    L --> B
    C --> M[Read Optimal Solution from Final Tableau]
    G --> N[Report Infeasibility]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph InputPhase["INPUT PHASE"]
        LPP[Linear Programming Problem] --> CONVERT[Convert to Standard Form]
        CONVERT --> TABLEAU[Construct Initial Simplex Tableau]
    end

    subgraph ValidationPhase["VALIDATION PHASE"]
        TABLEAU --> CHECK1{Dual Feasible: All c_j minus z_j Less Than or Equal to 0?}
        CHECK1 -- No --> ERR1[Not Applicable for Dual Simplex. Use Primal Simplex.]
        CHECK1 -- Yes --> CHECK2{Primal Infeasible: Any b_i less than 0?}
        CHECK2 -- No --> OPT[Already Optimal. Stop.]
    end

    subgraph IterationPhase["ITERATION PHASE"]
        CHECK2 -- Yes --> LEAVE[Select Leaving Variable: Most Negative b_i]
        LEAVE --> RATIO[Compute Dual Ratio Test]
        RATIO --> ENTER[Select Entering Variable: Minimum Ratio]
        ENTER --> PIVOT[Perform Pivot Operation]
        PIVOT --> UPDATE[Update Tableau]
        UPDATE --> CHECK2
    end

    OPT --> OUTPUT[OUTPUT: Optimal Solution and Z Value]
    ERR1 --> OUTPUT
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q1 -- Less Than Equal --> Q2{Artificial Variables Needed?}

    Q2 -- No --> PRIMSIM[Use Primal Simplex]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: State the conditions under which the Dual Simplex Method can be applied to solve an LPP.**

**Model Answer:**

> The Dual Simplex Method can be applied when the following conditions are satisfied:
> 1. The LPP is in canonical form with **all constraints expressed as equalities** with non-negative variables.
> 2. The objective function is in **maximization** form.
> 3. There exists an **initial basic solution that is dual feasible** (all reduced costs $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ for non-basic variables).
> 4. The current basic solution is **primal infeasible** (at least one RHS value $b_i < 0$).
> 5. Typical application: problems with $\geq$ constraints where, after multiplying by $-1$, the RHS becomes negative, providing a ready dual-feasible starting basis.

**[3 Marks — Standard KTU Valuation]**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: How is the entering variable selected in the Dual Simplex Method? Explain with the dual ratio test formula.**

**Model Answer:**

> In the Dual Simplex Method, the **entering variable** is selected using the **dual ratio test**:
> 
> **Step 1:** After identifying the leaving row $r$ (row with most negative RHS), examine all non-basic variables in this row.
> 
> **Step 2:** Consider only those non-basic variables with **negative coefficients** ($a_{rj} < 0$) in row $r$.
> 
> **Step 3:** Compute the ratio for each such variable:
> $$\text{Ratio}_j = \frac{\vert c_j - z_j \vert}{\vert a_{rj} \vert}$$
> 
> **Step 4:** The non-basic variable with the **minimum ratio** enters the basis.
> 
> **Special case:** If no negative coefficient exists in the leaving row, the LPP has **no feasible solution**.

**[3 Marks — Standard KTU Valuation]**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Solve the following LPP using the Dual Simplex Method:**

$$\text{Maximize } Z = 2x_1 + 3x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$2x_1 + x_2 \geq 8$$
$$x_1 + 2x_2 \geq 10$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

#### **Part (a)** — Formulate and Solve the LPP using Dual Simplex [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert constraints by multiplying by $-1$:**

$$-2x_1 - x_2 \leq -8$$
$$-x_1 - 2x_2 \leq -10$$

**Step 2: Add slack variables $s_1, s_2$:**

$$-2x_1 - x_2 + s_1 = -8$$
$$-x_1 - 2x_2 + s_2 = -10$$

**Step 3: Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-8$ |
| $s_2$ | $-1$ | $-2$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-10$ |
| $Z$-row | $-2$ | $-3$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 4: Iteration 1**

- **Leaving variable:** $s_2$ (RHS = $-10$ is most negative)
- **Entering variable candidates:** $x_1$ and $x_2$ (both have negative coefficients)
  - Ratio for $x_1$: $\frac{|-2|}{|-1|} = 2$
  - Ratio for $x_2$: $\frac{|-3|}{|-2|} = 1.5$
  - **Minimum ratio = $1.5$ → $x_2$ enters**
- **Pivot element:** $a_{22} = -2$

**Pivot Operations:**

New Row 2 = Old Row 2 $\div (-2)$: $[0.5, 1, 0, -0.5, 5]$

New Row 1 = Old Row 1 + New Row 2: $[-1.5, 0, 1, -0.5, -3]$

New Z-row = Old Z-row + $3 \times$ New Row 2: $[-0.5, 0, 0, -1.5, 15]$

**Updated Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $-1.5$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-0.5$ | $-3$ |
| $x_2$ | $0.5$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $5$ |
| $Z$-row | $-0.5$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-1.5$ | $15$ |

**Step 5: Iteration 2**

- **Leaving variable:** $s_1$ (RHS = $-3$ is most negative)
- **Entering variable candidates:** $x_1$ ($-1.5$) and $s_2$ ($-0.5$) — both negative
  - Ratio for $x_1$: $\frac{|-0.5|}{|-1.5|} = 0.333$
  - Ratio for $s_2$: $\frac{|-1.5|}{|-0.5|} = 3$
  - **Minimum ratio = $0.333$ → $x_1$ enters**
- **Pivot element:** $a_{11} = -1.5$

**Pivot Operations:**

New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $\div (-1.5)$: $[1, 0, -0.667, 0.333, 2]$

New Row 2 = Old Row 2 $- 0.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, 1, 0.333, -0.667, 4]$

New Z-row = Old Z-row + $0.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, 0, -0.333, -1.333, 16]$

**Final Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-0.667$ | $0.333$ | $2$ |
| $x_2$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0.333$ | $-0.667$ | $4$ |
| $Z$-row | $0$ | $0$ | $-0.333$ | $-1.333$ | $16$ |

All RHS values are $\geq 0$. **Optimal solution reached!**

$$\boxed{x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 4, \quad Z_{\max} = 16}$$

#### **Part (b)** — Verify the Solution Graphically and Discuss Dual Simplex Advantages [7 Marks]

**Graphical Verification:**

The constraints form a feasible region in the first quadrant. The optimal point $(2, 4)$ lies at the intersection of the two binding constraints:
- $2x_1 + x_2 = 8$ → $(2, 4)$: $2(2) + 4 = 8$ ✓
- $x_1 + 2x_2 = 10$ → $(2, 4)$: $2 + 2(4) = 10$ ✓

**Advantages of Dual Simplex Method:**

1. **Avoids Artificial Variables:** Unlike Big-M and Two-Phase methods, no artificial variables are needed.
2. **Computational Efficiency:** Often converges faster for problems with many constraints and few variables.
3. **Sensitivity Analysis:** Ideal for re-optimization when RHS vector changes.
4. **Integer Programming:** Used as sub-routine in branch-and-bound.
5. **Cleaner Tableaus:** Starting basis is already in place; only feasibility needs restoration.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Solve the following LPP using the Dual Simplex Method:**

$$\text{Minimize } Z = 4x_1 + 5x_2$$
$$\text{Subject to:}$$
$$3x_1 + 2x_2 \geq 6$$
$$2x_1 + 4x_2 \geq 8$$
$$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

#### **Part (a)** — Convert to Standard Maximization Form and Apply Dual Simplex [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Convert Minimization to Maximization**

By duality principle: $\min Z = -\max(-Z)$

Alternatively, work directly. The standard approach for Dual Simplex with minimization:

Multiply the objective by $-1$: Maximize $Z' = -4x_1 - 5x_2$

**Step 2: Convert Constraints**

Multiply each constraint by $-1$:
$$-3x_1 - 2x_2 \leq -6$$
$$-2x_1 - 4x_2 \leq -8$$

Add slack variables:
$$-3x_1 - 2x_2 + s_1 = -6$$
$$-2x_1 - 4x_2 + s_2 = -8$$

**Step 3: Initial Tableau**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $-3$ | $-2$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-6$ |
| $s_2$ | $-2$ | $-4$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-8$ |
| $Z'$-row | $-4$ | $-5$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Dual Feasibility Check:** All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ ✓ ($-4, -5, 0, 0$)

**Step 4: Iteration 1**

- **Leaving:** $s_2$ (RHS = $-8$, most negative)
- **Entering candidates:** $x_1$ ($-2$), $x_2$ ($-4$)
  - Ratio for $x_1$: $\frac{|-4|}{|-2|} = 2$
  - Ratio for $x_2$: $\frac{|-5|}{|-4|} = 1.25$
  - **Min = $1.25$ → $x_2$ enters**
- **Pivot:** $a_{22} = -4$

**Pivot Operations:**

New Row 2 = Old Row 2 $\div (-4)$: $[0.5, 1, 0, -0.25, 2]$

New Row 1 = Old Row 1 + $2 \times$ New Row 2: $[-2, 0, 1, -0.5, -2]$

New $Z'$-row = Old $Z'$-row + $5 \times$ New Row 2: $[-1.5, 0, 0, -1.25, 10]$

**Updated Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | $-2$ | $0$ | $1$ | $-0.5$ | $-2$ |
| $x_2$ | $0.5$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-0.25$ | $2$ |
| $Z'$-row | $-1.5$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-1.25$ | $10$ |

**Step 5: Iteration 2**

- **Leaving:** $s_1$ (RHS = $-2$)
- **Entering candidates:** $x_1$ ($-2$), $s_2$ ($-0.5$)
  - Ratio for $x_1$: $\frac{|-1.5|}{|-2|} = 0.75$
  - Ratio for $s_2$: $\frac{|-1.25|}{|-0.5|} = 2.5$
  - **Min = $0.75$ → $x_1$ enters**
- **Pivot:** $a_{11} = -2$

**Pivot Operations:**

New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $\div (-2)$: $[1, 0, -0.5, 0.25, 1]$

New Row 2 = Old Row 2 $- 0.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, 1, 0.25, -0.375, 1.5]$

New $Z'$-row = Old $Z'$-row + $1.5 \times$ New Row 1: $[0, 0, -0.75, -0.875, 11.5]$

**Final Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $0.25$ | $1$ |
| $x_2$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0.25$ | $-0.375$ | $1.5$ |
| $Z'$-row | $0$ | $0$ | $-0.75$ | $-0.875$ | $11.5$ |

All RHS $\geq 0$ ✓. **Optimal!**

$$x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 1.5, \quad Z'_{\max} = 11.5$$

Converting back: $Z_{\min} = -Z'_{\max} = -11.5$

Wait, we need to be careful. Since we had $\min Z = 4x_1 + 5x_2$ and we maximized $Z' = -4x_1 - 5x_2$, the minimum is:
$$Z_{\min} = -Z'_{\max} = -11.5$$

**Verification:**
- $3(1) + 2(1.5) = 6 \geq 6$ ✓
- $2(1) + 4(1.5) = 8 \geq 8$ ✓
- $Z = 4(1) + 5(1.5) = 4 + 7.5 = 11.5$ ✓

$$\boxed{x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 1.5, \quad Z_{\min} = 11.5}$$

#### **Part (b)** — Compare Dual Simplex with Primal Simplex Method [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Primal Simplex | Dual Simplex |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Feasible to primal, may be infeasible to dual | Feasible to dual, may be infeasible to primal |
| **Direction** | Moves toward dual feasibility | Moves toward primal feasibility |
| **Entering Variable** | Most positive $c_j - z_j$ | Min ratio of $|c_j - z_j| / |a_{rj}|$ |
| **Leaving Variable** | Min ratio of $b_i / a_{is}$ for $a_{is} > 0$ | Most negative $b_i$ |
| **Termination** | All $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ | All $b_i \geq 0$ |
| **Use** | Direct LPP solving | Sensitivity, ≥ constraints |

**Key Difference:** Primal Simplex handles ≤ constraints naturally; Dual Simplex is preferred for ≥ and = constraints and for re-optimization after parameter changes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in Pivot Row Selection:** Students often forget to multiply constraints by $-1$ before applying Dual Simplex. Always ensure RHS is initially **non-positive** for at least one constraint to trigger Dual Simplex.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Ratio Test Direction:** For Dual Simplex, the ratio is $\frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|}$ where $a_{rj} < 0$. Do **NOT** use the same ratio formula as Primal Simplex.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the "No Negative Coefficient" Case:** If no column in the leaving row has a negative coefficient, the LPP is **infeasible**, not unbounded. This is a critical distinction.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Minimization:** When given a min problem, students forget to negate the objective. The sign conversion must be done before constructing the tableau.
> 
> 5. **Not Checking Dual Feasibility First:** Always verify that all $c_j - z_j \leq 0$ in the initial tableau. If not, you cannot apply Dual Simplex directly.
> 
> 6. **Pivot Arithmetic Mistakes:** A single sign error in Gauss-Jordan elimination cascades through the entire tableau. Double-check pivot row division before adjusting other rows.
> 
> 7. **Confusing Slack and Surplus Variables:** In ≥ constraints, you need **surplus** variables ($-s_i$). After multiplying by $-1$, these become slack variables. Mislabeling loses 1-2 marks immediately.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dual Simplex Method**
> 
> **Core Concept:**
> - ✅ Dual Simplex starts with a **dual-feasible, primal-infeasible** solution.
> - ✅ Goal: Restore **primal feasibility** while maintaining **dual optimality**.
> - ✅ Termination: All RHS values $b_i \geq 0$.
> 
> **Algorithm Steps (Memorize):**
> 1. ✅ Convert LPP to standard form (maximization, equality constraints).
> 2. ✅ Construct initial tableau with slack variables as basis.
> 3. ✅ Verify dual feasibility ($c_j - z_j \leq 0$ for all $j$).
> 4. ✅ If any $b_i < 0$, identify **leaving variable** = most negative RHS row.
> 5. ✅ For the leaving row, identify **entering variable** using min of $\frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|}$ over negative $a_{rj}$.
> 6. ✅ Perform pivot on $a_{rs}$ and repeat.
> 
> **Key Formulas:**
> - ✅ Leaving row: $\min\{b_i \mid b_i < 0\}$
> - ✅ Entering column: $\min\left\{\frac{|c_j - z_j|}{|a_{rj}|} : a_{rj} < 0\right\}$
> 
> **When to Use:**
> - ✅ LPPs with $\geq$ or $=$ constraints.
> - ✅ Sensitivity analysis (RHS changes).
> - ✅ Re-optimization after constraint additions.
> - ✅ Integer programming sub-problems.
> 
> **Special Cases:**
> - ✅ All RHS $\geq 0$ → Optimal.
> - ✅ No negative coefficient in leaving row → **Infeasible LPP**.
> - ✅ All $c_j - z_j = 0$ → Multiple optimal solutions.
> 
> **Avoid These Mistakes:**
> - ❌ Using Primal Simplex ratio formula in Dual Simplex.
> - ❌ Forgetting sign conversion of $\geq$ constraints.
> - ❌ Not checking dual feasibility before starting.
> - ❌ Missing arithmetic in Gauss-Jordan elimination.
> 
> **Comparison Hooks for Exams:**
> - Primal vs. Dual Simplex: Mirror-image methods (entering/selection rules swap).
> - Dual Simplex vs. Big-M: Dual Simplex avoids artificial variables.
> - Dual Simplex vs. Two-Phase: Dual Simplex is faster for ≥ constraints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Two-Phase Method** is a systematic algorithmic procedure in Linear Programming (LP) used to determine an initial **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** when a starting BFS cannot be identified through conventional methods (such as the graphical method or the slack-variable technique alone). It was developed as a robust alternative to the Big-M Method, particularly suited for solving problems where constraints are of mixed types (≥, =, ≤) or when artificial variables are inevitably introduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A two-stage computational procedure in which **Phase I** formulates and solves an auxiliary linear programming problem to drive artificial variables to zero (thereby eliminating infeasibility), and **Phase II** uses the resultant BFS as the starting point to solve the original linear programming problem.

### The Two Phases Explained

**Phase I — Auxiliary Problem (Feasibility Check):**
- Formulate a new objective function: Minimize $\sum A_i$ where $A_i$ are the artificial variables.
- Solve this auxiliary LP using the Simplex Method.
- **If the optimal value of Phase I = 0** → All artificial variables are driven out of the basis → A feasible BFS to the original problem has been found.
- **If the optimal value of Phase I > 0** → At least one artificial variable remains positive in the basis → The original problem has **NO FEASIBLE SOLUTION** (infeasible LPP).

**Phase II — Original Problem Optimization:**
- Discard the Phase I objective function.
- Use the BFS from Phase I as the **initial simplex tableau** for the original problem.
- Re-introduce the original objective function (Z = cᵀx) and continue with the standard Simplex Method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Big-M Method:** Unlike the Big-M method, the Two-Phase Method avoids assigning arbitrarily large penalty constants (M), which often lead to numerical instability and rounding errors in computer implementations. Hence, it is the **preferred computational approach** in modern LP solvers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Rescue Mission"**
> Imagine you are a **mountain rescuer** trying to reach a stranded hiker on a cliff. The path has many hidden obstacles (≥ constraints, equality constraints), so you cannot simply walk in. You must first send a **scout team** to check if a safe path exists. The scout's only job is to clear the obstacles and report back. Once the scout confirms the path is passable, you (the main rescue team) begin your actual mission.

In this analogy:
- **The Scout Team** = Phase I (with the auxiliary objective of removing artificial barriers).
- **The Main Rescue Mission** = Phase II (solving the real optimization problem).
- **The Hidden Obstacles** = Artificial variables ($A_i$).
- **The Hiker (your real goal)** = The original objective function (Z).

If the scout team comes back and says, *"Sorry, no path exists — the cliff is unclimbable"*, then there is no point starting the main mission. Mathematically, if Phase I's optimal value is **strictly positive**, the original LPP has no feasible region to optimize over.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Artificial Variables?

| Constraint Type | Standard Slack/Surplus Action | Artificial Variable Required? |
|:----------------|:------------------------------|:------------------------------|
| $\leq$ (less than or equal) | Add **Slack variable** $S_i \geq 0$ | ❌ No |
| $\geq$ (greater than or equal) | Subtract **Surplus variable** $S_i \geq 0$ | ✅ Yes (to start BFS) |
| $=$ (equality) | No slack/surplus added | ✅ Yes (to start BFS) |

**Slack variables** are naturally ≥ 0 and form a valid initial BFS. **Surplus variables** and equality constraints do not provide a natural identity matrix in the coefficient matrix, so **artificial variables** ($A_i$) are added solely to construct an identity basis. They must be eliminated by the end of Phase I.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Form Assumption:** Before applying the Two-Phase Method, all constraints must be expressed in equality form. Maximization problems should be converted to minimization (or the cost row adjusted accordingly) to maintain a consistent simplex tableau structure.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Recap

| Term | Meaning |
|:-----|:--------|
| **BFS** | Basic Feasible Solution — a vertex of the feasible region |
| **Artificial Variable ($A_i$)** | A dummy variable introduced to form an initial basis, with no physical meaning |
| **Auxiliary Problem** | The Phase I problem whose objective is $\min \sum A_i$ |
| **Phase I Optimal Value** | The minimum sum of artificial variables; if $>0$, original LPP is infeasible |
| **Basis Carry-Over** | The transfer of the final basis from Phase I to Phase II |
| **Infeasibility** | A condition where no point satisfies all constraints simultaneously |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The Two-Phase Method operates on a foundational principle of **decomposition of infeasibility**. The core logical flow is:

1. **Why introduce artificial variables?**
   - The Simplex Method requires a starting BFS. With ≥ and = constraints, the origin $(0, 0, \ldots, 0)$ is **not feasible** because surplus variables are negative when constraints are ≥.
   - Artificial variables create a *trivially feasible* starting point at the origin.

2. **Why use a separate Phase I?**
   - Artificial variables must be **forced to zero** in the final solution because they have no economic meaning.
   - The auxiliary objective $\min \sum A_i$ mathematically guarantees this elimination.

3. **Why discard the Phase I cost row?**
   - Once infeasibility is resolved, the *artificial* objective is irrelevant. The real objective (Z) takes over.

4. **Why can Phase I have $>0$ optimum?**
   - If at least one artificial variable remains positive in the optimal Phase I basis, it means some constraints are **mutually contradictory**, leaving the feasible region empty.

---

## 2.2 Structured Step-by-Step Algorithm

### **Phase I — Auxiliary Problem**

- **Step 1:** Convert all constraints to equality form by adding slack/surplus variables.
- **Step 2:** Add artificial variables $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m$ to constraints that lack an obvious basic variable (i.e., those with surplus variables or equality type).
- **Step 3:** Formulate the **auxiliary objective function**:
$$W = \sum_{i=1}^{m} A_i = A_1 + A_2 + \cdots + A_m$$
- **Step 4:** Express W as a linear function of the non-basic variables by substituting out the artificial variables using the constraint equations. This ensures the tableau's $C_j - Z_j$ row is correctly computed.
- **Step 5:** Apply the **standard Simplex Method** to solve $\min W$.
- **Step 6:** Examine the result:
  - If $\min W = 0$ and all $A_i = 0$ → **Proceed to Phase II**.
  - If $\min W > 0$ → **STOP**: Original LPP is infeasible. No solution exists.

### **Phase II — Original Problem**

- **Step 7:** Take the final Phase I tableau. Remove the artificial variable columns.
- **Step 8:** Re-introduce the **original objective function** $Z = c^T x$ and compute the new $C_j - Z_j$ row.
- **Step 9:** If any artificial variable is still in the basis at value 0 (degenerate case), pivot it out using a column with a non-zero coefficient.
- **Step 10:** Apply the **standard Simplex Method** to optimize the original objective (max or min).
- **Step 11:** Report the optimal solution $(x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*)$ and the optimal $Z^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Degeneracy in Two-Phase Method:** If an artificial variable is basic with value zero at the end of Phase I, it must be pivoted out in Phase II (using any column with a non-zero coefficient in its row). Otherwise, the simplex iterations may stall in a degenerate cycle.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Usage |
|:--|:--------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $W = \sum_{i=1}^{m} A_i$ | Phase I auxiliary objective (minimize sum of artificials) |
| 2 | $\min W = 0$ | Necessary condition to proceed to Phase II |
| 3 | $\min W > 0$ | Original LPP is infeasible |
| 4 | $C_j - Z_j = C_j - \sum (C_B \cdot a_{ij})$ | Net evaluation row in simplex |
| 5 | $\text{Entering variable} = \max (C_j - Z_j)$ (for max) | Pivot column selection (max problem) |
| 6 | $\text{Entering variable} = \min (C_j - Z_j)$ (for min) | Pivot column selection (min problem) |
| 7 | $\theta = \min \left( \dfrac{X_B}{x_{ij}} \right), \quad x_{ij} > 0$ | Minimum ratio test (leaving variable) |
| 8 | $Z^* = C_B^T \cdot X_B$ | Optimal objective value from basis |
| 9 | $x_B = B^{-1} b$ | Basic variable values |
| 10 | $A_i \geq 0$ | Constraint on all artificial variables |
| 11 | $A_i = 0$ in final solution | Required for validity of BFS from Phase I |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid Symbol Confusion:** In simplex tables, $C_j$ represents the objective coefficient of variable $j$, $C_B$ is the cost vector of the current basis, $X_B$ is the solution column, and $Z_j$ is the opportunity cost. Do not confuse with artificial variable $A_i$ in Phase I.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Two-Phase Method is foundational to **operations research software** used in:

- **Supply Chain & Logistics:** Production planning where warehouse capacity has both minimum ($\geq$) and maximum ($\leq$) bounds.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Mission planning with hard equality constraints (e.g., fuel-mass balance).
- **Telecommunications:** Network flow optimization with demand and supply equality constraints.
- **Energy Systems:** Power dispatch problems with both minimum generation limits and maximum capacity limits.
- **Manufacturing:** Blending problems with strict composition requirements (equality constraints on ingredient ratios).

The Two-Phase Method's numerical stability (no arbitrary $M$ values) makes it ideal for large-scale **commercial LP solvers** like CPLEX, Gurobi, and open-source variants.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model KTU Board Question):**
> Solve the following Linear Programming Problem using the **Two-Phase Method**:
> $$\text{Maximize } Z = 3x_1 + x_2$$
> Subject to:
> $$2x_1 + x_2 \geq 2$$
> $$x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 3$$
> $$x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

---

### **Step 1: Convert Constraints to Standard Form**

- **Constraint 1** ($2x_1 + x_2 \geq 2$): Subtract surplus $S_1$ and add artificial $A_1$:
$$2x_1 + x_2 - S_1 + A_1 = 2$$
- **Constraint 2** ($x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 3$): Add slack $S_2$:
$$x_1 + 3x_2 + S_2 = 3$$

So the standard form is:
$$2x_1 + x_2 - S_1 + A_1 = 2$$
$$x_1 + 3x_2 + S_2 = 3$$

**Variables:** $x_1, x_2, S_1, S_2, A_1 \geq 0$ (5 variables, 2 constraints → 3 non-basic, 2 basic).

---

### **Step 2: Formulate Phase I Auxiliary Problem**

$$W_{\min} = A_1 \quad (\text{since only } A_1 \text{ is artificial})$$

Express $W$ in terms of non-basic variables by substituting the row equation:
$$A_1 = 2 - 2x_1 - x_2 + S_1$$
$$W = A_1 = 2 - 2x_1 - x_2 + S_1$$

Equivalently:
$$W + 2x_1 + x_2 - S_1 = 2$$

The coefficients in the W-equation (for simplex row) are: $2, 1, 0, -1, 0$ for $x_1, x_2, S_1, S_2, A_1$.

---

### **Step 3: Initial Phase I Simplex Tableau**

| Basis | $C_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | $\theta$ Ratio |
|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:----------------|
| $A_1$ | 1 | 2 | 2 | 1 | -1 | 0 | 1 | $2/2 = 1$ |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $3/1 = 3$ |
| $W$-row | | 2 | 2 | 1 | -1 | 0 | 0 | |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | | | 2 | 1 | -1 | 0 | 0 | |

> [!NOTE]
> The $C_j - Z_j$ row already accounts for the W-equation coefficients. Here, the most positive is **2** (for $x_1$), so $x_1$ enters the basis.

---

### **Step 4: First Pivot — Bring $x_1$ into the Basis**

- **Pivot Column:** $x_1$ (largest $C_j - Z_j = 2$)
- **Pivot Row:** $A_1$ row (smallest $\theta = 1$)
- **Pivot Element:** 2

Perform row operations to make the pivot column an identity:

**New $x_1$ row** (divide by 2): $(1, \ 1/2, \ -1/2, \ 0, \ 1/2, \ \vert \ 1)$

**New $S_2$ row** = Old $S_2$ row $-$ (1 × New $x_1$ row):
$$(3 - 1, \ 3 - 1/2, \ 0 - (-1/2), \ 1 - 0, \ 0 - 1/2 \ \vert \ 3 - 1)$$
$$= (2, \ 5/2, \ 1/2, \ 1, \ -1/2 \ \vert \ 2)$$

**New $W$-row** = Old $W$-row $-$ (2 × New $x_1$ row):
$$(2 - 2, \ 1 - 1, \ -1 - (-1), \ 0 - 0, \ 0 - 1 \ \vert \ 2 - 2)$$
$$= (0, \ 0, \ 0, \ 0, \ -1 \ \vert \ 0)$$

Updated Tableau:

| Basis | $C_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | $\theta$ |
|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:---------:|
| $x_1$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1/2 | -1/2 | 0 | 1/2 | — |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 2 | 0 | 5/2 | 1/2 | 1 | -1/2 | — |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | -1 | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$** (for minimization) and **$\min W = 0$**. Also, $A_1$ has been driven out of the basis. ✅ **Phase I complete — feasible BFS obtained.**

---

### **Step 5: Phase II Setup — Re-introduce Original Objective**

The BFS from Phase I is: $x_1 = 1, \ S_2 = 2, \ x_2 = 0, \ S_1 = 0, \ A_1 = 0$.

Original objective: $Z_{\max} = 3x_1 + x_2$

For Phase II, we need $C_B$ corresponding to the new basis $(x_1, S_2)$:
$$C_B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

Compute $C_j - Z_j$ for all non-basic variables:
$$C_j - Z_j = C_j - C_B^T \cdot (\text{column } j)$$

For $x_2$: $C_j - Z_j = 1 - (3 \cdot 1/2 + 0 \cdot 5/2) = 1 - 3/2 = -1/2$

For $S_1$: $C_j - Z_j = 0 - (3 \cdot (-1/2) + 0 \cdot 1/2) = 0 + 3/2 = 3/2$

For $A_1$: $C_j - Z_j = 0 - (3 \cdot 1/2 + 0 \cdot (-1/2)) = -3/2$

> **Note:** The $A_1$ column is no longer needed (since $A_1 = 0$ and is non-basic). It can be dropped from the Phase II tableau.

Phase II Initial Tableau:

| Basis | $C_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $\theta$ |
|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:---------:|
| $x_1$ | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1/2 | -1/2 | 0 | $1/(1/2) = 2$ |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 2 | 0 | 5/2 | 1/2 | 1 | $2/(5/2) = 4/5$ |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | | | 0 | -1/2 | 3/2 | 0 | |

- **Most positive $C_j - Z_j$** is $3/2$ for $S_1$. So $S_1$ enters the basis.
- **Minimum ratio** is $4/5$ for $S_2$. So $S_2$ leaves.
- **Pivot element:** $1/2$.

---

### **Step 6: Second Pivot — Bring $S_1$ into the Basis**

**New $S_1$ row** (divide $S_2$ row by 1/2 = multiply by 2):
$(0, \ 5, \ 1, \ 2, \ \vert \ 4)$

**New $x_1$ row** = Old $x_1$ row $-$ $(-1/2)$ × New $S_1$ row = Old + $(1/2)$ × New:
$$x_1 \text{ row: } \Big(1 + 0, \ 1/2 + 5/2, \ -1/2 + 1, \ 0 + 1 \ \Big\vert \ 1 + 2\Big) = (1, \ 3, \ 0, \ 1 \ \vert \ 3)$$

Updated Tableau:

| Basis | $C_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $\theta$ |
|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:------|:---------:|
| $x_1$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | 3 | 0 | 1 | — |
| $S_1$ | 0 | 4 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 2 | — |
| $C_j - Z_j$ | | | 0 | -2 | 0 | -3 | |

> [!NOTE]
> All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ → **Optimal solution reached.** Optimality verified.

---

### **Step 7: Final Answer**

From the optimal tableau:
$$x_1^* = 3, \quad x_2^* = 0, \quad S_1 = 4, \quad S_2 = 0$$
$$Z^* = 3(3) + 1(0) = 9$$

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Solution: } x_1 = 3, \ x_2 = 0, \ Z_{\max} = 9}$$

---

## 3.2 Infeasibility Case — Worked Illustration

> [!TIP]
> **Alternate Scenario — When Phase I Fails:**
> If the original problem had the constraint $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 1$ *in addition* to $2x_1 + x_2 \geq 2$, the constraints would be **mutually contradictory**. In this case, even after Phase I iterations, $\min W > 0$, and we conclude: **"The LPP has no feasible solution; hence no optimal solution exists."**
