Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 3, \lambda_2 = 1$, both positive $\Rightarrow$ $H_f \succ 0 \Rightarrow$ **strictly convex** objective.

*Step 5:* State final canonical NLP:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min\ & f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + x_1 x_2 - 10 x_1 - 12 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.}\ & g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 8 \le 0 \\
& g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 2 - x_1 \le 0 \\
& x_1 \ge 0,\ x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 KTU-Style Worked Numerical Problem (Model Solution)

**Question:** Formulate the following engineering design problem as an NLP and classify it.

> *Minimize the weight of a cylindrical pressure vessel* (volume $V$ fixed) where the radius $r$ and height $h$ are decision variables. The surface area to be minimized is $A = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$, with constraints $V = \pi r^2 h = 5$ (fixed) and $r, h \ge 0$.

**Solution:**

*Step 1 — Decision Variables:* $\mathbf{x} = (r, h)^T$.

*Step 2 — Objective Function:* $\min A(r, h) = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h$.

*Step 3 — Equality Constraint:* $h(\pi r^2) = 5 \Rightarrow \pi r^2 h - 5 = 0$.

*Step 4 — Bound Constraints:* $r \ge 0,\ h \ge 0$.

*Step 5 — Final NLP Formulation:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min\ & f(r, h) = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h \\
\text{s.t.}\ & h_1(r,h) = \pi r^2 h - 5 = 0 \\
& r \ge 0,\ h \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 6 — Classification:* This is a **linearly constrained NLP** with one equality constraint. Using the constraint to eliminate $h = 5/(\pi r^2)$ reduces it to a **single-variable unconstrained NLP** in $r$.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always perform the **6-step formulation template** — (i) identify variables, (ii) state objective, (iii) list constraints, (iv) convert to canonical $\le 0$ form, (v) compute Hessian for convexity, (vi) classify the NLP type. Examiners reward every step explicitly.

### 3.4 Convexity Verification — Step-by-Step Procedure

For a twice-differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$:

1. Compute the gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ (vector of first partial derivatives).
2. Compute the Hessian matrix $H_f(\mathbf{x})$ (matrix of second partial derivatives).
3. Check if $H_f(\mathbf{x})$ is **positive semidefinite** for all $\mathbf{x}$:
   * Compute principal minors (Sylvester's criterion) — all $\ge 0$.
   * Or compute eigenvalues — all $\ge 0$.
4. If yes $\Rightarrow$ $f$ is convex; else non-convex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A positive *definite* Hessian means **strictly** convex $\Rightarrow$ unique global minimum. A positive *semidefinite* Hessian means convex but flat in some directions $\Rightarrow$ may have a non-unique minimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Optimization Problems (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Optimization Problem] --> LIN[Linear Programming LP]
    ROOT --> NLP[Non Linear Programming NLP]
    LIN --> LP1[Simplex Method]
    LIN --> LP2[Interior Point Method]
    NLP --> UN[Unconstrained NLP]
    NLP --> CN[Constrained NLP]
    UN --> UN1[Single Variable Methods]
    UN --> UN2[Multivariable Methods]
    UN1 --> UN1A[Golden Section]
    UN1 --> UN1B[Newton Raphson]
    UN2 --> UN2A[Steepest Descent]
    UN2 --> UN2B[Newton Method]
    UN2 --> UN2C[Conjugate Gradient]
    CN --> CN1[Equality Only]
    CN --> CN2[Inequality Included]
    CN1 --> CN1A[Lagrange Multiplier]
    CN1 --> CN1B[Substitution]
    CN2 --> CN2A[KKT Conditions]
    CN2 --> CN2B[Penalty Method]
    CN2 --> CN2C[Barrier Method]
    CN2 --> CN2D[Sequential QP]
    CN2A --> CN2A1[Convex NLP]
    CN2A --> CN2A2[Non Convex NLP]
```

### 4.2 NLP Formulation Workflow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Decision Variables] --> B[State Objective Function]
    B --> C[List All Constraints]
    C --> D[Convert to Canonical Form]
    D --> E[Compute Hessian Matrix]
    E --> F[Check Convexity]
    F --> G{Convex or Not}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply KKT Sufficient Conditions]
    G -- No --> I[Apply Numerical Methods]
    H --> J[Global Optimum Guaranteed]
    I --> K[Local Optimum via Heuristics]
    J --> L[Final Solution x star]
    K --> L
```

### 4.3 NLP Solution Space (Conceptual Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FEAS[Feasible Region S] --> OBJ[Objective Contour Levels]
    OBJ --> C1[Level Set C1 Highest Cost]
    OBJ --> C2[Level Set C2]
    OBJ --> C3[Level Set C3]
    OBJ --> C4[Level Set C4 Lowest Cost]
    C4 --> STAR[Optimal Point x star]
    FEAS --> EXT[Extreme Points of S]
    EXT -.may coincide.-> STAR
    FEAS --> INT[Interior Points of S]
    INT -.may coincide.-> STAR
    FEAS --> BND[Boundary Points of S]
    BND -.may coincide.-> STAR
```

### 4.4 LP vs. NLP Feasible Region Comparison (Text Schematic)

* **LP Feasible Region:** A **convex polygon** (or polyhedron in higher dimensions). Optimum is at a **vertex** of this polygon.
* **NLP Feasible Region:** A **curved, possibly non-convex set**. The boundary may be a smooth curve, a saddle-shaped surface, or even disconnected. Optimum can be at a smooth interior point, on a curved boundary, or at a sharp corner.

```
  LP Optimum                NLP Optimum
  at a Vertex              on a Curved Boundary
       *                       *
      /|\                     /\
     / | \                   /  \   (curve)
    /  |  \                 /    \
   *---+---*               *------*  (feasible region is curved)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Non-Linear Programming Problem (NLP). Give one example of an NLP from engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **NLP** is an optimization problem in which the objective function or at least one of the constraints (or both) is a non-linear function of the decision variables.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, where at least one of $f, g_i, h_j$ is non-linear.
>
> **Engineering example:** Optimal power flow in a power system, where the power balance equations are $P_i = V_i \sum_j V_j (G_{ij} \cos\theta_{ij} + B_{ij} \sin\theta_{ij})$ — a trigonometric, non-linear function of voltage angles and magnitudes.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three differences between Linear Programming (LP) and Non-Linear Programming (NLP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Objective Function:** LP has a linear objective $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$; NLP has a non-linear objective $f(\mathbf{x})$.
> 2. **Feasible Region:** LP's feasible region is a convex polyhedron with an optimum at a vertex; NLP's feasible region is a (possibly non-convex) curved set with the optimum possibly in the interior.
> 3. **Solution Method:** LP is solved by the Simplex/Interior-Point method in polynomial time; NLP requires iterative numerical methods (Newton, SQP, KKT) and may have multiple local optima.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the general formulation of a Non-Linear Programming Problem. List the four essential components of any NLP. (7 marks)

**(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products $x_1$ and $x_2$. The profit function is $P = 6x_1 + 8x_2 - x_1^2 - 0.5 x_2^2$. The total available raw material is 10 units, with $x_1 + x_2 \le 10$, and the market can absorb at least 1 unit of each. Formulate this as a standard NLP in canonical form. Check convexity using the Hessian. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A general NLP is mathematically written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min\ & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{s.t.}\ & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ i = 1, \ldots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0,\ j = 1, \ldots, p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

The **four essential components** are:
1. **Decision variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)^T$.
2. **Objective function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ (scalar measure of performance).
3. **Constraints** $g_i$ (inequalities) and $h_j$ (equalities).
4. **Feasible region** $S$ = set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Variables:* $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^T$.

*Step 2 — Objective:* Maximize $P = 6x_1 + 8x_2 - x_1^2 - 0.5 x_2^2$, equivalently:

$$
\min f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + 0.5 x_2^2 - 6 x_1 - 8 x_2
$$

*Step 3 — Constraints (in canonical $\le 0$ form):*
$$
x_1 + x_2 - 10 \le 0
$$
$$
1 - x_1 \le 0, \quad 1 - x_2 \le 0
$$
$$
-x_1 \le 0, \quad -x_2 \le 0
$$

*Step 4 — Hessian Matrix:*
$$
H_f = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

*Step 5 — Convexity Check:* Eigenvalues are $2$ and $1$, both positive $\Rightarrow H_f \succ 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **strictly convex objective**. The problem is a **convex NLP**, and any local minimum is the global minimum.

*Final NLP Formulation:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min\ & x_1^2 + 0.5 x_2^2 - 6 x_1 - 8 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.}\ & x_1 + x_2 - 10 \le 0 \\
& 1 - x_1 \le 0,\quad 1 - x_2 \le 0 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Classify the different types of Non-Linear Programming Problems. Explain any four categories with suitable examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the NLP: $\min f(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$ subject to $x_1^2 - x_2 \le 0$ and $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$. Formulate the Lagrangian and state the KKT conditions for this problem. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

NLPs are classified based on the nature of the objective and constraints:

1. **Unconstrained NLP** — No constraints; e.g., $\min (x^2 + 2x + 1)$.
2. **Quadratic Programming (QP)** — Quadratic objective with linear constraints; e.g., portfolio optimization $\min \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T \Sigma \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \mathbf{x}$ s.t. $\sum x_i = 1,\ x_i \ge 0$.
3. **Convex NLP** — Convex objective over a convex set; e.g., $\min x_1^2 + x_2^2$ s.t. $x_1 + x_2 \ge 1$.
4. **Geometric Programming (GP)** — Posynomial objective and constraints; e.g., minimize transistor delay in VLSI.
5. **Fractional Programming** — Ratio objective; e.g., cost/benefit ratios in finance.
6. **Separable Programming** — Objective is a sum of single-variable functions.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 — Lagrangian:*
$$
L(x_1, x_2, \lambda_1, \mu_1, \mu_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 + \lambda_1 (x_1^2 - x_2) + \mu_1 (-x_1) + \mu_2 (-x_2)
$$

*Step 2 — KKT Stationarity:*
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 2) + 2\lambda_1 x_1 - \mu_1 = 0
$$
$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 1) - \lambda_1 - \mu_2 = 0
$$

*Step 3 — Primal Feasibility:*
$$
x_1^2 - x_2 \le 0,\quad x_1 \ge 0,\quad x_2 \ge 0
$$

*Step 4 — Dual Feasibility:*
$$
\lambda_1 \ge 0,\quad \mu_1 \ge 0,\quad \mu_2 \ge 0
$$

*Step 5 — Complementary Slackness:*
$$
\lambda_1 (x_1^2 - x_2) = 0,\quad \mu_1 x_1 = 0,\quad \mu_2 x_2 = 0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the maximization objective** when converting to minimization (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Not writing constraints in the canonical $\le 0$ form** — the problem must always have $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, not $\ge 0$ (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Skipping the Hessian computation** in the convexity check — a question that says "check convexity" *requires* you to compute the matrix and the eigenvalues (or principal minors) explicitly (lose 2 marks).
> 4. **Missing complementary slackness in KKT** — many students write stationarity and feasibility but forget $\lambda_i g_i = 0$ (lose 1 mark).
> 5. **Vague definitions of NLP** — stating "non-linear means not linear" without mathematical form $f, g_i, h_j$ gets only 1 of 3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Definition:** An NLP is an optimization problem with at least one non-linear component in the objective, constraints, or both.
* **Canonical Form:** $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
* **Four Components:** Decision variables, objective function, constraints, feasible region.
* **Convexity Test:** A twice-differentiable $f$ is convex iff its Hessian is positive semidefinite ($H_f \succeq 0$).
* **Convex NLP advantage:** Local minimum = Global minimum (unique solution, efficient computation).
* **Non-convex NLP challenge:** Multiple local optima; global optimization is NP-hard in general.
* **KKT Conditions:** Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness — must be satisfied at the optimum under a constraint qualification (LICQ).
* **NLP vs. LP:** LP has a polyhedral feasible region with vertex optima; NLP has a curved region with optima possibly anywhere.
* **Special NLP Subclasses:** QP, Convex NLP, GP, Separable Programming, Fractional Programming.
* **Formulation Template (6 steps):** Identify variables → State objective → List constraints → Convert to canonical form → Compute Hessian → Classify and check convexity.
* **Constraint Tricks:** Lower bound $x \ge a$ becomes $a - x \le 0$; upper bound $x \le b$ becomes $x - b \le 0$.
* **Engineering Applications:** Optimal power flow, chemical reactor design, structural optimization, portfolio management, VLSI transistor sizing, trajectory optimization.
* **Slater's Condition:** For convex NLPs, strict feasibility ensures strong duality holds.
* **Solution Methods (Preview for next topics):** Lagrange Multipliers, KKT, Penalty/Barrier Methods, Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP), Augmented Lagrangian, Interior-Point Methods.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** Memorize the canonical NLP form, the Lagrangian expression, the four KKT conditions, and the Hessian-based convexity test. These four items alone account for **~70%** of marks in any NLP formulation question. Always write constraints in $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ form to avoid losing easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convex and Non-Convex Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convex and Non-Convex Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Convex Function

Let $f : S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a real-valued function defined on a **convex set** $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$. The function $f$ is said to be **convex** on $S$ if, for every pair of points $x_1, x_2 \in S$ and for every scalar $\lambda \in [0,1]$, the following inequality holds:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Geometric Interpretation (KTU Board Key Point)**
> The line segment joining any two points on the graph of a convex function always lies **on or above** the graph. Algebraically, the chord lies above the curve.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of a Concave Function

A function $f$ is **concave** on $S$ if $-f$ is convex on $S$. Equivalently:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \ge \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> [!NOTE] **Convex vs. Concave Quick Memory Hook**
> Convex = "cup-shaped" (∪) — bowl holding water
> Concave = "cap-shaped" (∩) — dome shedding water

### 1.3 Formal Definition of a Non-Convex Function

A function is classified as **non-convex** if there exists at least one pair $x_1, x_2 \in S$ and some $\lambda \in (0,1)$ such that the convexity inequality is violated:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) > \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

> [!NOTE] **Strictly Convex / Strictly Concave**
> If the inequality holds strictly (with $\lambda \in (0,1)$ and $x_1 \neq x_2$) for every pair, then $f$ is **strictly convex** (or strictly concave). This property guarantees **uniqueness of the global minimum** — a board-exam favourite.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are hiking across a landscape:

- A **convex function** is like a smooth **valley** (think of a smooth river basin). No matter which two points you pick on the valley walls, the straight line connecting them hovers *above* the terrain. The **bottom of the valley is the global minimum** — and it is the *only* lowest point.
- A **non-convex function** is like the **Western Ghats of Kerala** — full of peaks, dips, ridges, and valleys. A straight line drawn between two mountain peaks might cut straight through the earth (i.e., lie below the surface). Here, you can get stuck in a small foothill (local minimum) and never find the deepest valley (global minimum).

> [!TIP] **Why KTU Examiners Love This Topic**
> Convexity is the *single most important structural property* in optimisation. It guarantees that **every local minimum is a global minimum**, which is why convex programming is a central pillar of Module 3.

### 1.5 The Epigraph — A Geometric Companion

The **epigraph** of a function $f$ is the set of all points lying on or above its graph:

$$\text{epi}(f) = \{ (x, t) \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1} \; : \; x \in S, \; t \ge f(x) \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Fundamental Theorem (Board Favourite)**
> A function $f$ is convex **if and only if** its epigraph $\text{epi}(f)$ is a **convex set**.
> This is often asked as a 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE papers.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Six Step-Wise Properties

**Step 1 — Restriction to a Line:**  
A multivariable function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is convex if and only if its restriction to *every* line in the domain is convex. Mathematically, $g(t) = f(x + t d)$ must be convex in $t$ for every $x$ and direction $d$.

**Step 2 — First-Order Condition (Differentiable Case):**  
If $f$ is differentiable, then $f$ is convex **iff** for all $x, y \in S$:

$$f(y) \ge f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x)$$

The right-hand side is the equation of the **tangent hyperplane** at $x$. Geometrically: the entire graph of $f$ lies on or above its tangent at *any* point.

**Step 3 — Second-Order Condition (Twice-Differentiable Case):**  
If $f \in C^2$, then $f$ is convex **iff** the **Hessian matrix** $\nabla^2 f(x)$ is **positive semi-definite** for all $x \in S$:

$$\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0 \quad \forall x \in S$$

> [!NOTE] **Hessian Test for 2 Variables**
> For $f(x, y)$: convexity holds iff $f_{xx} \ge 0$, $f_{yy} \ge 0$, and $f_{xx} f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 \ge 0$ (principal minors non-negative).

**Step 4 — Jensen's Inequality (Generalised Form):**  
If $f$ is convex, then for any finite collection of points $x_i \in S$ and weights $\lambda_i \ge 0$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i = 1$:

$$f\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i x_i \right) \le \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i f(x_i)$$

**Step 5 — Subgradient Inequality (Non-Differentiable Case):**  
A vector $g$ is a subgradient of $f$ at $x_0$ if:

$$f(y) \ge f(x_0) + g^T (y - x_0) \quad \forall y \in S$$

The set of all such $g$ is the **subdifferential** $\partial f(x_0)$, which is non-empty for convex functions.

**Step 6 — Operations Preserving Convexity:**

| Operation | Result |
|---|---|
| Non-negative scaling ($\alpha f$, $\alpha \ge 0$) | Convex |
| Sum of convex functions | Convex |
| Affine composition: $h(x) = f(Ax + b)$ | Convex if $f$ is convex |
| Pointwise maximum: $f(x) = \max_i f_i(x)$ | Convex |
| Pointwise supremum | Convex |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Use the table below as your **last-night revision cheat sheet** for Module 3.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Domain/Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convex Function (Definition) | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | Concave Function | $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \ge \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ | $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 3 | First-Order Condition | $f(y) \ge f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T (y - x)$ | Differentiable $f$ |
| 4 | Second-Order Condition | $\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0$ (PSD) | Twice-differentiable $f$ |
| 5 | Epigraph Test | $\text{epi}(f)$ is a convex set | Always |
| 6 | Jensen's Inequality | $f\left(\sum \lambda_i x_i\right) \le \sum \lambda_i f(x_i)$ | $\sum \lambda_i = 1,\ \lambda_i \ge 0$ |
| 7 | Subgradient Definition | $f(y) \ge f(x_0) + g^T (y - x_0)$ | $g \in \partial f(x_0)$ |
| 8 | Hessian Determinant Test (2D) | $f_{xx} \ge 0,\ f_{yy} \ge 0,\ f_{xx}f_{yy} - f_{xy}^2 \ge 0$ | $f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| 9 | Fenchel Conjugate | $f^*(y) = \sup_x \{ y^T x - f(x) \}$ | Always |
| 10 | Strict Convexity | Strict inequality for $x_1 \neq x_2$, $\lambda \in (0,1)$ | Stronger than convexity |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Systems

Convex and non-convex function classification is the **bedrock** of modern engineering decision-making:

- **Portfolio Optimisation (Finance Engineering):** Minimising risk subject to return constraints reduces to a convex program; risk functions like variance are quadratic (convex).
- **Machine Learning:** Training loss functions in **Support Vector Machines** and **Logistic Regression** are convex; loss landscapes in deep neural networks are notoriously *non-convex*.
- **Power Systems:** Optimal power flow is reformulated into a **semi-definite program** (convex) for tractability.
- **Structural Design:** Compliance minimisation in truss design uses convex relaxations of non-convex stress constraints.
- **Signal Processing:** $\ell_1$-regularisation (LASSO) exploits the convexity of $\vert x \vert$ to promote sparsity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Proving $f(x) = x^2$ is Convex

We must show that for any $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the left-hand side using the definition $f(x) = x^2$:**

$$f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) = [\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2]^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the algebraic identity $(a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
[\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2]^2 &= \lambda^2 x_1^2 + 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 + (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) &= \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the LHS from the RHS to check non-negativity of the difference:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2 - [\lambda^2 x_1^2 + 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 + (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2]
&= \lambda x_1^2 - \lambda^2 x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2 - (1-\lambda)^2 x_2^2 - 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 \\
&= \lambda(1-\lambda) x_1^2 + (1-\lambda)\lambda x_2^2 - 2\lambda(1-\lambda) x_1 x_2 \\
&= \lambda(1-\lambda) \left[ x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2 x_1 x_2 \right] \\
&= \lambda(1-\lambda) (x_1 - x_2)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude using non-negativity of the factors:**

Since $\lambda \in [0,1]$ gives $\lambda(1-\lambda) \ge 0$ and $(x_1 - x_2)^2 \ge 0$, the product is non-negative:

$$\lambda(1-\lambda)(x_1 - x_2)^2 \ge 0$$

**Step 6 — Final Verdict:**

$$\therefore f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2) \quad \blacksquare$$

Hence $f(x) = x^2$ is **convex** on $\mathbb{R}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE] **Alternative Proof via Second-Order Condition**
> $f'(x) = 2x$ and $f''(x) = 2 > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. Therefore the Hessian (which is just the scalar $2$) is positive definite everywhere, so $f$ is strictly convex.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Proving $f(x) = e^x$ is Convex

**Step 1 — Write the convexity condition:**

$$e^{\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2} \le \lambda e^{x_1} + (1-\lambda) e^{x_2}$$

**Step 2 — Observe that $\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2$ is a linear combination; factor it out as a power:**

$$e^{\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2} = e^{\lambda x_1} \cdot e^{(1-\lambda) x_2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the geometric mean form (AM-GM approach).** Apply the weighted AM-GM inequality:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda a + (1-\lambda) b &\ge a^\lambda \cdot b^{(1-\lambda)} \quad \text{for } a, b \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $a = e^{x_1}$ and $b = e^{x_2}$ (both positive):

$$\lambda e^{x_1} + (1-\lambda) e^{x_2} \ge (e^{x_1})^\lambda \cdot (e^{x_2})^{(1-\lambda)} = e^{\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging yields:**

$$e^{\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2} \le \lambda e^{x_1} + (1-\lambda) e^{x_2}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** $f(x) = e^x$ is **convex** on $\mathbb{R}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Proving $f(x) = x^3$ is Non-Convex Globally

**Step 1 — Test the second-order condition.** Compute:

$$f'(x) = 3x^2, \quad f''(x) = 6x$$

**Step 2 — Check the sign of $f''$ over the domain:**

- For $x > 0$: $f''(x) = 6x > 0$ → **convex** on $(0, \infty)$.
- For $x < 0$: $f''(x) = 6x < 0$ → **concave** on $(-\infty, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Verdict:** Since $f''(x)$ changes sign and is negative on the left half-line, $f(x) = x^3$ fails the global second-order convexity test. It is **not convex** on all of $\mathbb{R}$.

**Step 4 — Counterexample.** Take $x_1 = -2$, $x_2 = 1$, $\lambda = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(0.5 \cdot (-2) + 0.5 \cdot 1) &= f(-0.5) = (-0.5)^3 = -0.125 \\
0.5 \cdot f(-2) + 0.5 \cdot f(1) &= 0.5 \cdot (-8) + 0.5 \cdot 1 = -4 + 0.5 = -3.5
\end{aligned}$$

Since $-0.125 > -3.5$, the inequality $f(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \le \lambda f(x_1) + (1-\lambda) f(x_2)$ is **violated** — confirming $f(x) = x^3$ is **non-convex globally**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Is $f$ Convex?

Use this flowchart logic when you encounter a problem in the exam:

| Test | When to Use | Condition for Convexity | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition Test** | Any function | Chord always above graph | Forgetting to check *all* pairs |
| **First-Order Test** | Differentiable | Tangent line always below graph | Miscomputing the gradient |
| **Second-Order Test** | $C^2$ smooth | $\nabla^2 f(x) \succeq 0 \ \forall x$ | Only checking determinant, not trace |
| **Epigraph Test** | Any function | $\text{epi}(f)$ is a convex set | Confusing with subgraph |
| **Composition Test** | Composite functions | Outer non-decreasing + inner convex | Forgetting monotonicity |
| **Jensen's Test** | Inequalities | $f(\mathbb{E}[X]) \le \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ | Wrong direction of inequality |

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Epigraph of $f(x) = x^2$ is Convex

**Step 1 — Write the epigraph definition:**

$$\text{epi}(f) = \{ (x, t) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : t \ge x^2 \}$$

**Step 2 — Take any two points $(x_1, t_1), (x_2, t_2) \in \text{epi}(f)$**, so $t_1 \ge x_1^2$ and $t_2 \ge x_2^2$.

**Step 3 — Form a convex combination with $\lambda \in [0,1]$:**

$$\lambda(x_1, t_1) + (1-\lambda)(x_2, t_2) = (\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2,\ \lambda t_1 + (1-\lambda) t_2)$$

**Step 4 — Show the new $t$-coordinate is $\ge$ the new $x$-coordinate squared:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda t_1 + (1-\lambda) t_2 &\ge \lambda x_1^2 + (1-\lambda) x_2^2 \\
&\ge [\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2]^2 \quad \text{(by convexity of } x^2 \text{, proved in 3.1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude:** The convex combination lies in $\text{epi}(f)$, so $\text{epi}(f)$ is a convex set, confirming $f(x) = x^2$ is convex. $\blacksquare$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Classifying a Function as Convex or Non-Convex

The following Mermaid flow diagram captures the decision process a student or researcher follows when classifying an arbitrary function. This mirrors the KTU Examiner's checklist.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Function f of x Given]) --> domCheck{Domain S Convex?}
    domCheck -- No --> invalidOut[Inconclusive: Test on Restricted Domain]
    domCheck -- Yes --> twiceDiff{Twice Differentiable?}
    twiceDiff -- Yes --> hessianCheck[Compute Hessian H equals grad squared f]
    hessianCheck --> eigenTest{All Eigenvalues of H Non Negative?}
    eigenTest -- Yes --> convexOut[CONVEX FUNCTION]
    eigenTest -- No --> defineTest
    twiceDiff -- No --> defineTest[Apply Chord Definition Test]
    defineTest --> chordTest{Chord Above Graph for ALL x1 x2 in S?}
    chordTest -- Yes --> convexOut
    chordTest -- No --> checkConcave{Negation of f Convex?}
    checkConcave -- Yes --> concaveOut[CONCAVE FUNCTION]
    checkConcave -- No --> nonConvexOut[NON CONVEX FUNCTION]
    invalidOut --> defineTest
    convexOut --> strictCheck{Strict Inequality for x1 Not Equal to x2?}
    strictCheck -- Yes --> strictOut[STRICTLY CONVEX]
    strictCheck -- No --> weakOut[WEAKLY CONVEX]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the Diagram**
> Begin at the top circle (startNode), follow the diamond decision nodes, and end at one of the four terminal classifications: `convexOut`, `concaveOut`, `nonConvexOut`, or `strictOut`. The `hessianCheck` branch is the fastest path for $C^2$ functions.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of Convexity in an Optimisation Pipeline

This diagram shows how the convexity test feeds into the **broader optimisation workflow** — a high-yield visual for the 14-mark questions where students must justify their algorithmic choice.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    problemIn([Engineering Problem]) --> formStep[Formulate as Math Model]
    formStep --> classifyStep{Convex or Non Convex?}
    classifyStep -- Convex --> pathA[Select Deterministic Solver]
    pathA --> kktA[Apply KKT Conditions]
    kktA --> globalA[Single Global Optimum Guaranteed]
    classifyStep -- Non Convex --> pathB[Apply Heuristic or Metaheuristic]
    pathB --> metaB[Simulated Annealing GA Particle Swarm]
    metaB --> localB[Multiple Local Optima Possible]
    globalA --> finalOut[Result]
    localB --> finalOut[Result]
```

> [!TIP] **How to Use in the Exam**
> If a 14-mark question asks "Why is convexity important in optimisation?", draw this flow and annotate it. Examiners reward **flowchart-style solutions** with bonus marks for visual clarity.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Convex vs. Non-Convex Graphical Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sideA[CONVEX FUNCTION SHAPE] --> bowlNote[Bowl or Valley Geometry]
    sideA --> chordNote1[Chord Always Above Curve]
    sideA --> optNote1[Unique Global Minimum]
    sideA --> exampleNote1[Examples: x squared, e to x, absolute x]
    sideB[NON CONVEX FUNCTION SHAPE] --> mountainNote[Multi Peak Valley Geometry]
    sideB --> chordNote2[Chord May Dip Below Curve]

```
