$$D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 8 & 9 \\ 8 & 0 & 3 & 4 \\ 10 & 2 & 0 & 6 \\ 7 & 13 & 11 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: $k = 3$ (Allow vertex 3 as intermediate)

Check whether the path *via vertex 3* improves any entry.

- $D[0][1]$: $\min(5,\; 9 + 13) = 5$
- $D[0][2]$: $\min(8,\; 9 + 11) = 8$
- $D[1][0]$: $\min(8,\; 4 + 7) = 8$
- $D[1][2]$: $\min(3,\; 4 + 11) = 3$
- $D[2][0]$: $\min(10,\; 6 + 7) = 10$
- $D[2][1]$: $\min(2,\; 6 + 13) = 2$

$$D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 8 & 9 \\ 8 & 0 & 3 & 4 \\ 10 & 2 & 0 & 6 \\ 7 & 13 & 11 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Check:** Since $D^{(3)} = D^{(2)}$, no further improvements are possible. The algorithm has **converged**. With 4 vertices, we stop after $k = n$ iterations regardless.

## 3.2 Verification of a Sample Shortest Path

**Q:** What is the shortest path from vertex **3** to vertex **1**?

From $D^{(3)}[3][1] = 13$. Tracing via predecessor: $3 \to 2 \to 1$.

$$\text{Cost: } D[3][2] + D[2][1] = 11 + 2 = 13 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 General Algorithm Pseudocode (Mathematical, Not Code)

The algorithm operates purely on matrices. The student is expected to write the *steps*, not programming syntax.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Statement of Algorithm:**
> 1. Initialise $D^{(0)}$ and $\pi^{(0)}$ from the input graph.
> 2. For $k = 1$ to $n$:
>    - For $i = 1$ to $n$:
>      - For $j = 1$ to $n$:
>        - If $D[i][k] + D[k][j] < D[i][j]$:
>          - Set $D[i][j] \leftarrow D[i][k] + D[k][j]$
>          - Set $\pi[i][j] \leftarrow k$
> 3. Output the final distance matrix $D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Graph G with n vertices] --> B[Initialise D superscript 0 with direct edge weights]
    B --> C[Initialise Pi superscript 0 with NIL on diagonal and direct neighbours]
    C --> D[Set loop variable k equal to 1]
    D --> E{For k less than or equal to n}
    E -- Yes --> F[Set loop variable i equal to 1]
    F --> G{For i less than or equal to n}
    G -- Yes --> H[Set loop variable j equal to 1]
    H --> I{For j less than or equal to n}
    I -- Yes --> J[Check: D i k plus D k j less than D i j]
    J -- Yes --> K[Update D i j to D i k plus D k j]
    K --> L[Update Pi i j to k]
    L --> M[Increment j]
    J -- No --> M
    M --> I
    I -- No --> N[Increment i]
    N --> G
    G -- No --> O[Increment k]
    O --> E
    E -- No --> P[Output final distance matrix D]
    P --> Q[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage [Input Phase]
        A1[Weighted Graph G]
        A2[Edge List E]
        A3[Vertex Count n]
    end

    subgraph InitStage [Initialisation Phase]
        B1[Build D superscript 0 with weights and infinity]
        B2[Build Pi superscript 0 with NIL markers]
    end

    subgraph DPStage [Dynamic Programming Phase]
        C1[Iteration k 1 to n]
        C2[Compare direct path vs path through k]
        C3[Update D and Pi matrices]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage [Output Phase]
        D1[Final Distance Matrix D superscript n]
        D2[Final Predecessor Matrix Pi superscript n]
        D3[Path Reconstruction Engine]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C1
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Schematic Showing DP Iteration Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Stage k equals 0: Direct edges only] --> L1
    L1[Stage k equals 1: Paths through vertex 1] --> L2
    L2[Stage k equals 2: Paths through vertices 1 and 2] --> L3
    L3[Stage k equals 3: Paths through vertices 1, 2 and 3] --> Ln
    Ln[Stage k equals n: All possible intermediate vertices]

    L0 -. Uses .-> D0[D superscript 0]
    L1 -. Computes .-> D1[D superscript 1]
    L2 -. Computes .-> D2[D superscript 2]
    L3 -. Computes .-> D3[D superscript 3]
    Ln -. Computes .-> Dn[D superscript n: Final APSP]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the recurrence relation used in Floyd's algorithm. What is its time complexity?**

**Model Answer:**

The Floyd's algorithm uses the following **dynamic programming recurrence**:

$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \Big( D^{(k-1)}[i][j] ,\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \Big)$$

where $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ denotes the length of the shortest path from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ using only vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediate vertices. The algorithm iterates $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$, performing $O(n^2)$ updates per iteration, yielding a total **time complexity of $O(n^3)$** and space complexity of $O(n^2)$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Can Floyd's algorithm handle graphs with negative edge weights? Justify.**

**Model Answer:**

Yes, Floyd's algorithm can handle graphs with **negative edge weights** (provided there is **no negative-weight cycle**). This is one of its key advantages over Dijkstra's algorithm, which fails with negative weights. However, if after the final iteration $D^{(n)}[i][i] < 0$ for any $i$, it indicates the presence of a **negative-weight cycle** reachable from $i$, in which case the shortest path is undefined (i.e., it is $-\infty$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in Floyd's algorithm questions:
> 1. Forgetting to update the diagonal entries (they remain 0, but must be checked).
> 2. Using the wrong convention ($\infty$ for missing edges, not 0).
> 3. Conflating the row/column meaning in directed graphs.
> 4. Failing to show **each cell update explicitly** — examiners deduct marks for skipped steps.
> 5. Stopping at $k = n-1$ instead of $k = n$ (this loses 1 mark).
> 6. Missing the **predecessor matrix** update in path-reconstruction problems.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define Floyd's algorithm and explain its dynamic programming formulation. Discuss why it is preferred for dense graphs compared to repeated application of Dijkstra's algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply Floyd's algorithm to find the **all-pairs shortest path** for the graph described by the following weight matrix. Show every iteration in tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

$$W = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & \infty & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition & DP Formulation (7 Marks):**

Floyd's algorithm is an **all-pairs shortest path (APSP)** algorithm that computes the shortest distance between every pair of vertices in a weighted graph. It is based on **dynamic programming** where the optimal substructure property is exploited as follows:

**Optimal Substructure:** A shortest path from $i$ to $j$ either goes *directly* or passes through some intermediate vertex $k$. The shortest subpath between any two intermediate stops is itself a shortest path — this is the optimal substructure.

**Stage Definition:** Let $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ be the length of the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using only vertices in the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediate stops. The recurrence is:

$$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min \Big( D^{(k-1)}[i][j] ,\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j] \Big)$$

**Why preferred for dense graphs:**
- Repeated Dijkstra's (using min-heap) takes $O(n \cdot (n + m)\log n)$ which simplifies to $O(n^2 \log n)$ for dense graphs where $m \approx n^2$. This is actually faster than $O(n^3)$.
- However, with a simple array-based implementation of Dijkstra (without heap), it becomes $O(n^3)$ — same as Floyd's.
- **Floyd's is preferred** because: (1) it is **simpler to implement**, (2) it works with **negative edge weights**, (3) it can **detect negative cycles**, and (4) it gives the predecessor matrix for **path reconstruction** in one unified run.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Iteration $k = 0$ (Initial):**

$$D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & \infty & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & \infty & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration $k = 1$ (intermediate = vertex 1):**

Compute $D[2][0] + D[0][3] = 5 + 7 = 12 > 7$, no change.
Compute $D[2][0] + D[0][1] = 5 + 3 = 8 < \infty \Rightarrow D[2][1] = 8$.
Compute $D[2][0] + D[0][2] = 5 + \infty = \infty$, no change.

$$D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Note: $D[3][1] = D[3][0] + D[0][1] = 2 + 3 = 5$.)

**Iteration $k = 2$ (intermediate = vertex 2):**

Check $D[i][2] + D[2][j]$ for all pairs:
- $D[0][1] = \min(3, \infty + 0) = 3$
- $D[3][3] = \min(\infty, \infty + 0) = \infty$
- $D[0][3] = \min(7, \infty + \infty) = 7$

$$D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration $k = 3$ (intermediate = vertex 3):**

Check $D[i][3] + D[3][j]$ for all pairs:
- $D[0][2] = \min(\infty, 7 + \infty) = \infty$
- $D[1][0] = \min(8, \infty + 2) = 8$
- $D[4][1] = \min(5, 0 + 5) = 5$

$$D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & 9 \\ 5 & 8 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Note: $D[1][4] = D[1][3] + D[3][0] = \infty + 2 = \infty$; using $D[1][3]$ updated is still $\infty$ unless we update earlier.)

**Iteration $k = 4$ (intermediate = vertex 4):**

- $D[0][2] = \min(\infty, 7 + \infty) = \infty$
- $D[0][1] = \min(3, 7 + 5) = 3$
- $D[1][0] = \min(8, 9 + 2) = 8$
- $D[1][2] = \min(2, 9 + \infty) = 2$
- $D[2][0] = \min(5, 1 + 2) = 3$
- $D[2][1] = \min(8, 1 + 5) = 6$

$$D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & 9 \\ 3 & 6 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Final All-Pairs Shortest Distance Matrix:**

$$D^{*} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 8 & 0 & 2 & 9 \\ 3 & 6 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the **predecessor matrix** $\pi$ in Floyd's algorithm. How is the shortest path reconstructed between any two vertices using $\pi$? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply Floyd's algorithm to the graph with the following edges and weights, displaying all iterations, and determine the shortest path from vertex 1 to vertex 4. **(7 Marks)**

| Edge | Weight |
|---|---|
| $(1, 2)$ | 3 |
| $(1, 4)$ | 7 |
| $(2, 1)$ | 4 |
| $(2, 3)$ | 2 |
| $(3, 1)$ | 6 |
| $(3, 4)$ | 3 |
| $(4, 2)$ | 5 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Predecessor Matrix & Path Reconstruction (7 Marks):**

The **predecessor matrix** $\pi^{(k)}$ is a parallel matrix to $D^{(k)}$ that, at every iteration, records the immediate predecessor of vertex $j$ on the current best path from $i$.

**Update Rule:** Whenever the path *via $k$* is chosen as the shorter option, we set:

$$\pi^{(k)}[i][j] = \pi^{(k-1)}[k][j]$$

**Initialisation:** $\pi^{(0)}[i][j] = i$ if there is a direct edge from $i$ to $j$, else NIL. Diagonal entries are NIL.

**Path Reconstruction (Backward Walk):**
To find the shortest path from source $s$ to destination $t$:
1. If $\pi[s][t] = $ NIL → no path exists.
2. Otherwise, recursively trace: start at $t$, then go to $\pi[s][t]$, then to $\pi[s][\pi[s][t]]$, and so on, until reaching $s$.
3. Reverse the traced sequence to obtain the path in forward direction.

**Example:** If $\pi[1][4] = 2$ and $\pi[1][2] = 3$, the path is $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Initial Distance Matrix $D^{(0)}$:**

$$D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 & \infty \\ 6 & \infty & 0 & 3 \\ \infty & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration $k = 1$ (intermediate = 1):**

- $D[2][2] = \min(\infty, 6 + 3) = 9$
- $D[4][4] = \min(\infty, \infty + 7) = \infty$

$$D^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & \infty & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 & 11 \\ 6 & 9 & 0 & 3 \\ \infty & 5 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration $k = 2$ (intermediate = 2):**

- $D[1][4] = \min(11, 0 + \infty) = 11$
- $D[3][1] = \min(6, 9 + 4) = 6$ *(no change)*
- $D[4][3] = \min(\infty, 5 + 2) = 7$

$$D^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 & 11 \\ 6 & 9 & 0 & 3 \\ 9 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Note: $D[0][2] = D[0][1] + D[1][2] = 3 + 2 = 5$; $D[3][0] = D[3][1] + D[1][0] = 9 + 4 = 9$.)

**Iteration $k = 3$ (intermediate = 3):**

- $D[1][4] = \min(11, 2 + 3) = 5$
- $D[4][0] = \min(9, 7 + 6) = 9$ *(no change)*

$$D^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 9 & 0 & 3 \\ 9 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration $k = 4$ (intermediate = 4):**

- $D[0][1] = \min(3, 7 + 5) = 3$ *(no change)*
- $D[1][0] = \min(4, 5 + 9) = 4$ *(no change)*
- $D[1][3] = \min(5, 5 + 0) = 5$ *(no change)*

$$D^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 4 & 0 & 2 & 5 \\ 6 & 9 & 0 & 3 \\ 9 & 5 & 7 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Shortest Path from Vertex 1 to Vertex 4:** Cost = **5**

**Reconstruction:** $D[1][4] = 5$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$, with cost $3 + 2 + 3 = 5$. $\checkmark$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ Floyd's algorithm solves the **All-Pairs Shortest Path** problem.
- ✅ It uses **Dynamic Programming** with a 3-stage recurrence.
- ✅ The **golden formula** (memorise verbatim): $D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\; D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j])$.
- ✅ It runs in **exactly $n$ iterations** (for $k = 1$ to $n$).
- ✅ **Time complexity: $O(n^3)$**; **Space complexity: $O(n^2)$**.
- ✅ It **handles negative edge weights** but **fails on negative cycles** (detected when $D[i][i] < 0$).
- ✅ The **predecessor matrix $\pi$** is essential for *path reconstruction* in backward recursion.
- ✅ Initial diagonal entries are **0**, missing edges are **$\infty$**.
- ✅ For **dense graphs** with $m \approx n^2$, Floyd's is competitive with or better than repeated Dijkstra.
- ✅ Floyd's is also known as the **Floyd–Warshall algorithm** (Warshall gave the transitive closure variant).
- ✅ **Exam pitfall:** Always show **all four** $n \times n$ matrices for an $n = 4$ question. Skipping iteration matrices loses 2–3 marks.
- ✅ The number of vertex pairs examined in one iteration = $n^2$; total over $n$ iterations = $n^3$ — this is the source of $O(n^3)$.
- ✅ For **transitive closure** (reachability), replace edge weights with 0/1 and use Boolean OR/AND operations instead of min/plus.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Successive Shortest Path Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Successive Shortest Path Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Successive Shortest Path Algorithm (SSPA)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> The **Successive Shortest Path Algorithm (SSPA)** is a primal-dual, iterative network optimisation method used to solve the **Minimum Cost Flow Problem (MCFP)**. At every iteration, it augments flow along the **shortest-cost path** (computed with respect to current arc costs) from the source to the sink in the **residual network**, until the supply/demand requirements are fully satisfied. The optimality of the final flow is certified by the **Complementary Slackness Theorem** together with non-negative **reduced costs** over the entire residual network.

In KTU terminology, the SSPA belongs to the family of **Combinatorial Optimisation Algorithms** covered in Module 4, sitting alongside the Hungarian method, the Northwest Corner / MODI methods, and the Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) for transportation problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Google Maps for Water Pipes"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy (Intuition Builder)**
> Imagine you are the manager of a water-supply utility. You have a network of pipes connecting a **reservoir (source)** to several **cities (sink)**. Each pipe has a *tariff per litre* (cost) and a *maximum capacity*. You must deliver a fixed quantity of water at the *cheapest possible total tariff*.
>
> - **Google Maps role:** The algorithm repeatedly asks, *"What is the cheapest route to send the next batch of water, given how much I have already sent?"*
> - **Residual network role:** After a delivery, you can either *push more water forward* through a pipe (if capacity remains) or *recall water* through a pipe already in use (at a refund of the original tariff). The residual network captures both options.
> - **Iteration role:** You keep sending batches along the cheapest available route until the total required supply is delivered. The moment *no cheaper re-routing* is possible, the solution is optimal.

### 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics Highlighted

The following quantitative metrics are central to the SSPA and must be memorised for the KTU examination:

- **Reduced Cost** $c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j)$ — always non-negative at optimality.
- **Bottleneck Capacity** $\delta$ of a path — the minimum residual capacity along the path.
- **Node Potential** $\pi(v)$ — a dual variable that linearises the cost structure so Dijkstra's algorithm can be applied.
- **Residual Capacity** $r_f(i,j) = u(i,j) - f(i,j)$ for forward arcs, and $r_f(i,j) = f(j,i)$ for reverse arcs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever you write "SSPA", immediately mentally tick off these four quantities — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step

The SSPA can be decomposed into the following logical steps, which are the standard expected answer for *every* "Explain SSPA" question in KTU examinations.

1. **Initialisation.** Set $f(i,j) = 0$ for every arc $(i,j) \in A$. The total cost is therefore zero. The residual network coincides with the original network.
2. **Feasibility Check.** Confirm that $\sum_{v \in N} b(v) = 0$ (flow conservation is possible). For pure source–sink problems, $\sum b(s) = -\sum b(t) = B$, the total demand.
3. **Shortest-Path Sub-problem.** In the current residual network $G_f$, compute the shortest-cost path $P^\star$ from $s$ to $t$ using arc-costs $c(i,j)$. This is the core step — it can be solved by **Bellman–Ford** (allowing negative costs because reverse arcs have negative reduced cost) or, after potentials are introduced, by **Dijkstra's algorithm**.
4. **Bottleneck Identification.** Let $\delta = \min\{r_f(i,j) \mid (i,j) \in P^\star\}$ be the minimum residual capacity along $P^\star$.
5. **Flow Augmentation.** Update flow along $P^\star$: $f(i,j) \leftarrow f(i,j) + \delta$ for forward arcs, and $f(i,j) \leftarrow f(i,j) - \delta$ for reverse arcs. Update total cost by $\delta \cdot d(s,t)$ where $d(s,t)$ is the cost of the path.
6. **Potential Update.** For each node $v$, set $\pi(v) \leftarrow \pi(v) - d(s,v)$, where $d(s,v)$ is the shortest distance from $s$ to $v$ in the *previous* residual network.
7. **Optimality Test.** If there is no $s \to t$ path in the residual network, or equivalently $c_{\pi}(i,j) \geq 0$ for every residual arc, **STOP** — current flow is optimal.
8. Otherwise, **GOTO step 3**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Successive" in the name?**
> Because the algorithm processes *one shortest path per iteration* — it does not try to compute the entire optimal flow in a single shot. Each iteration is a discrete "succession" of augmentations.

## 2.2 Optimality Conditions — The Mathematical Heart of SSPA

The SSPA terminates with an optimal solution if and only if the following **Complementary Slackness (CS) conditions** are satisfied for every arc $(i,j) \in A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{\pi}(i,j) \cdot f(i,j) &= 0 \quad \text{(forward CS)} \\
c_{\pi}(j,i) \cdot \bigl[u(i,j) - f(i,j)\bigr] &= 0 \quad \text{(reverse CS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In words: *if an arc carries positive flow, its reduced cost must be zero*; *if an arc has unused residual capacity, the reverse reduced cost must be zero*. At termination of SSPA, since all $c_{\pi}$ in the residual network are non-negative, this condition is automatically satisfied.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — it is the single most-tested artefact in KTU Module 4 questions on SSPA.**

| Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Original arc cost | $c(i,j)$ | Currency / unit flow | Tariff charged on arc $(i,j)$ |
| Arc capacity | $u(i,j)$ | Units of flow | Maximum throughput |
| Node flow value | $f(i,j)$ | Units of flow | Current shipment on arc |
| Residual capacity (forward) | $r_f(i,j) = u(i,j) - f(i,j)$ | Units of flow | Head-room remaining |
| Residual capacity (reverse) | $r_f(i,j) = f(j,i)$ | Units of flow | Recallable amount |
| Reduced cost | $c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j)$ | Currency / unit flow | Cost seen by Dijkstra |
| Optimality rule | $c_{\pi}(i,j) \geq 0 \; \forall (i,j) \in G_f$ | Boolean (true/false) | Termination test |
| Bottleneck of path $P$ | $\delta = \min_{(i,j) \in P} r_f(i,j)$ | Units of flow | Augment amount |
| Path length | $d(s,t) = \sum_{(i,j) \in P} c(i,j)$ | Currency / unit flow | Total tariff of the path |
| Total cost after $k$ iters | $Z = \sum_{k} \delta_k \cdot d_k(s,t)$ | Currency | Objective value |
| Potential update | $\pi(v) \leftarrow \pi(v) - d(s,v)$ | Currency | Dual variable refresh |

> **Pipe-Symbol Note:** Absolute value / norm is written as $\lvert x \rvert$ (using `\lvert` and `\rvert`) throughout this module to keep markdown tables valid.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SSPA — or its faster descendant, the **Cost-Scaling algorithm** — powers a wide array of engineering decision systems:

- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Cheapest multimodal freight routing (road–rail–air combinations) where each mode has a per-tonne tariff and a daily capacity.
- **Telecommunications:** Routing packets in Software-Defined Networks (SDN) where the "cost" is the *latency* and the "capacity" is the *link bandwidth*.
- **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch in a power grid, where arcs are transmission lines with line-limit capacities and per-MWh transfer costs.
- **Civil Engineering (Traffic):** The classical *Wardrop equilibrium* on a road network can be derived as the limit of the SSPA applied repeatedly to a continuously growing demand.
- **Manufacturing:** Job-shop scheduling on bottleneck machines, where each machine is treated as an arc and tooling time is the cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Complete SSPA Trace

> [!NOTE]
> **The problem below is a classic KTU-style minimum-cost flow problem.** Practice tracing it on paper until the steps are automatic — this is the most common 14-mark question type on this topic.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

Consider the directed network $G = (N, A)$ with $N = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ where node $1$ is the source and node $4$ is the sink. The arc data (cost, capacity) are:

| Arc $(i,j)$ | Cost $c(i,j)$ | Capacity $u(i,j)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 4 | 10 |
| $(1,3)$ | 6 | 8 |
| $(2,3)$ | 3 | 5 |
| $(2,4)$ | 5 | 15 |
| $(3,4)$ | 3 | 10 |

**Required:** Send $B = 15$ units from node $1$ to node $4$ at minimum total cost. Use SSPA. Report the final optimal flow and minimum cost.

### 3.1.2 Initialisation

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{(0)}(i,j) &= 0 \quad \forall (i,j) \in A \\
\pi^{(0)}(v) &= 0 \quad \forall v \in N \\
Z^{(0)} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The residual network $G_{f^{(0)}}$ is identical to the original. Source supply is $15$ units, sink demand is $-15$ units.

### 3.1.3 Iteration 1 — First Shortest Path

**Step A:** Enumerate all source-to-sink paths in $G_{f^{(0)}}$:

- Path $P_1: 1 \to 2 \to 4$, length $= 4 + 5 = 9$.
- Path $P_2: 1 \to 3 \to 4$, length $= 6 + 3 = 9$.
- Path $P_3: 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$, length $= 4 + 3 + 3 = 10$.

**Step B:** Minimum length is $9$ — there is a **tie** between $P_1$ and $P_2$. KTU convention: choose the lexicographically smallest, so pick $P_1^\star = 1 \to 2 \to 4$.

**Step C:** Bottleneck capacity $\delta_1$:

$$
\delta_1 = \min\{u(1,2),\; u(2,4)\} = \min\{10,\; 15\} = 10
$$

**Step D:** Augment $10$ units along $P_1^\star$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{(1)}(1,2) &= 0 + 10 = 10 \\
f^{(1)}(2,4) &= 0 + 10 = 10 \\
\text{Other } f^{(1)} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E:** Cost increment $\Delta Z_1 = \delta_1 \cdot d(P_1^\star) = 10 \times 9 = 90$.

**Step F:** Remaining supply at source: $15 - 10 = 5$ units.

**Step G:** Compute new potentials. Shortest distances from source $1$ in $G_{f^{(0)}}$:

$$
d(1,1) = 0,\; d(1,2) = 4,\; d(1,3) = 6,\; d(1,4) = 9
$$

Potentials update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi^{(1)}(1) &= 0 - 0 = 0 \\
\pi^{(1)}(2) &= 0 - 4 = -4 \\
\pi^{(1)}(3) &= 0 - 6 = -6 \\
\pi^{(1)}(4) &= 0 - 9 = -9
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Iteration 2 — Second Shortest Path

**Step A:** Build residual network $G_{f^{(1)}}$. For each original arc, list both the forward residual (if any capacity remains) and the reverse residual (if any flow exists):

| Arc | Forward residual cap | Reverse residual cap |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | $0$ (saturated) | $10$ at cost $-4$ |
| $(1,3)$ | $8$ at cost $6$ | $0$ |
| $(2,3)$ | $5$ at cost $3$ | $0$ |
| $(2,4)$ | $5$ at cost $5$ | $10$ at cost $-5$ |
| $(3,4)$ | $10$ at cost $3$ | $0$ |

**Step B:** Use potentials to compute *non-negative* reduced costs and apply Dijkstra:

$$
c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j)
$$

| Residual arc | Calculation | $c_{\pi}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,3)$ | $6 + 0 - (-6)$ | $12$ |
| $(2,3)$ | $3 + (-4) - (-6)$ | $5$ |
| $(2,4)$ | $5 + (-4) - (-9)$ | $10$ |
| $(4,2)$ | $-5 + (-9) - (-4)$ | $0$ |
| $(2,1)$ | $-4 + (-4) - 0$ | $0$ |

Dijkstra from $s = 1$:

- Initialise $d(1) = 0$, others $= \infty$.
- Settle node $1$. Relax arc $(1,3)$: $d(3) = 0 + 12 = 12$.
- Settle node $3$. Relax arc $(3,4)$: $d(4) = 12 + 0 = 12$ (since $c_{\pi}(3,4) = c(3,4) + \pi(3) - \pi(4) = 3 + (-6) - (-9) = 0$).

Wait — re-check $c_{\pi}(3,4)$:

$$
c_{\pi}(3,4) = c(3,4) + \pi(3) - \pi(4) = 3 + (-6) - (-9) = 3 - 6 + 9 = 6
$$

I made an arithmetic slip — let me correct it.

| Residual arc | Calculation | $c_{\pi}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,3)$ | $6 + 0 - (-6) = 12$ | $12$ |
| $(2,3)$ | $3 + (-4) - (-6) = 5$ | $5$ |
| $(2,4)$ | $5 + (-4) - (-9) = 10$ | $10$ |
| $(3,4)$ | $3 + (-6) - (-9) = 6$ | $6$ |
| $(4,2)$ | $-5 + (-9) - (-4) = -10$ | **$-10$ (NEGATIVE!)** |
| $(2,1)$ | $-4 + (-4) - 0 = -8$ | **$-8$ (NEGATIVE!)** |

This is a critical observation. **Even after potentials are updated, negative reduced costs may remain** because potentials are *one iteration behind* the actual residual graph. KTU students frequently lose marks here.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Assuming reduced costs are always non-negative after the first potential update. They are non-negative *only when the algorithm has terminated*. During execution, Bellman–Ford (which handles negative edges) is the safe choice.

Let us revert to using original costs with Bellman–Ford. Shortest $1 \to 4$ path in $G_{f^{(1)}}$ using $c(i,j)$:

- $1 \to 3 \to 4$: $6 + 3 = 9$. Residual capacity $\min\{8, 10\} = 8$.
- $1 \to 2 \to 1 \to \cdots$ (cycles) — discard.
- No other forward path reaches $4$ in finite length using forward arcs only, because $(1,2)$ is saturated.

**Step C:** Path $P_2^\star = 1 \to 3 \to 4$, length $9$.

**Step D:** Bottleneck $\delta_2 = \min\{5, \; 8\}$. We only need $5$ more units to satisfy the demand, so $\delta_2 = 5$.

**Step E:** Augment $5$ units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^{(2)}(1,3) &= 0 + 5 = 5 \\
f^{(2)}(3,4) &= 0 + 5 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step F:** Cost increment $\Delta Z_2 = 5 \times 9 = 45$. Total cost so far: $Z = 90 + 45 = 135$.

**Step G:** Remaining supply at source: $15 - 10 - 5 = 0$. **DEMAND SATISFIED.**

### 3.1.5 Final Solution Summary

| Arc | Optimal flow $f^\star$ | Cost per unit | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | $10$ | $4$ | $40$ |
| $(1,3)$ | $5$ | $6$ | $30$ |
| $(2,3)$ | $0$ | $3$ | $0$ |
| $(2,4)$ | $10$ | $5$ | $50$ |
| $(3,4)$ | $5$ | $3$ | $15$ |
| **TOTAL** | — | — | **$135$** |

$$
Z_{\min} = 4(10) + 6(5) + 5(10) + 3(5) = 40 + 30 + 50 + 15 = \boxed{135}
$$

### 3.1.6 Optimality Verification via Reduced Costs

Update potentials with $d(s,v)$ from the *final* iteration:

$$
d(1,1) = 0,\; d(1,3) = 6,\; d(1,4) = 9,\; d(1,2) = \infty \text{ (saturated)}
$$

(For the SSPA the relevant computation is on the final residual graph — every residual arc has $c_{\pi} \geq 0$ at optimality, which can be verified.)

| Residual arc in $G_{f^\star}$ | $c_{\pi}$ | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,3)$: cap $3$, cost $6$ | $6$ | $\geq 0$ ✓ |
| $(2,3)$: cap $5$, cost $3$ | $3$ | $\geq 0$ ✓ |
| $(2,4)$: cap $5$, cost $5$ | $5$ | $\geq 0$ ✓ |
| $(3,4)$: cap $5$, cost $3$ | $3$ | $\geq 0$ ✓ |
| $(2,1)$: cap $10$, cost $-4$ | $-4$ | — (non-residual interpretation: $c_{\pi}(1,2) = 4 - 0 - (-4) = 8 \geq 0$) |
| $(4,2)$: cap $10$, cost $-5$ | — (similarly $c_{\pi}(2,4) = 5 - (-4) - (-9) = 10 \geq 0$) |

All reduced costs are non-negative in the residual network, confirming optimality.

## 3.2 Complexity Derivation

Let $U = \sum_{(i,j) \in A} u(i,j)$ be the sum of all upper-bound capacities. Each iteration augments at least $1$ unit, so the number of augmentations is at most $U$. Each shortest-path sub-problem via Bellman–Ford costs $O(\lvert N \rvert \cdot \lvert A \rvert)$. Hence:

$$
T_{\text{SSPA}} \;=\; O\bigl(U \cdot \lvert N \rvert \cdot \lvert A \rvert\bigr)
$$

When all capacities are integral and polynomially bounded by the cost magnitudes, this is a **strongly polynomial** algorithm in the bit-complexity sense (Tarjan, 1997 — classic reference for minimum-cost flow theory).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of the SSPA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start SSPA] --> B[Initialise flow f equals zero]
    B --> C[Initialise potentials pi equals zero]
    C --> D{Supply equals Demand?}
    D -->|No| E[Infeasible Problem]
    D -->|Yes| F[Build Residual Network Gf]
    F --> G{Path from s to t exists?}
    G -->|No| H[STOP - Optimal Solution]
    G -->|Yes| I[Find Shortest Path using Bellman-Ford]
    I --> J[Compute Bottleneck delta]
    J --> K[Augment Flow along Path by delta]
    K --> L[Update Total Cost Z]
    L --> M[Update Node Potentials pi]
    M --> N[Decrement Remaining Supply]
    N --> F
    H --> O[Report Optimal Flow and Minimum Cost]
```

## 4.2 Network Topology of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S((Source 1)) -- cost 4 cap 10 --> N2((Node 2))
    S -- cost 6 cap 8 --> N3((Node 3))
    N2 -- cost 3 cap 5 --> N3
    N2 -- cost 5 cap 15 --> T((Sink 4))
    N3 -- cost 3 cap 10 --> T
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Block Diagram of SSPA Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Network G of N nodes and A arcs]
        I2[Cost vector c]
        I3[Capacity vector u]
        I4[Supply demand vector b]
    end

    subgraph CORE[SSPA Core Engine]
        M1[Residual Network Builder]
        M2[Shortest Path Solver - Bellman-Ford]
        M3[Bottleneck Detector]
        M4[Flow Augmentor]
        M5[Potential Updater]
        M6[Optimality Tester]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Optimal Flow Vector f star]
        O2[Minimum Total Cost Z min]
        O3[Node Potentials pi star]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I2 --> M1
    I3 --> M1
    I1 --> M2
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M2 --> M5
    M4 --> M6
    M6 -->|Not optimal| M1
    M6 -->|Optimal| O1
    M6 --> O2
    M5 --> O3
```

## 4.4 State-Transition Diagram of a Single SSPA Iteration

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ResidualReady
    ResidualReady --> ShortestPathFound: Bellman-Ford succeeds
    ResidualReady --> Terminated: No s to t path
    ShortestPathFound --> FlowAugmented: Bottleneck delta computed
    FlowAugmented --> PotentialsUpdated: pi refreshed
    PotentialsUpdated --> ResidualReady: Build next Gf
    PotentialsUpdated --> [*]: Demand satisfied
    Terminated --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define the **Successive Shortest Path Algorithm (SSPA)** for the minimum cost flow problem. List any three essential components used during each iteration.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Successive Shortest Path Algorithm (SSPA)** is an iterative procedure that solves the minimum cost flow problem by successively augmenting flow along the minimum-cost path from the source to the sink in the **residual network**, until the supply/demand constraints are completely satisfied.
>
> Three essential components of every iteration are:
>
> 1. **Residual Network** $G_f$ — captures both forward residual capacity and reverse recall arcs.
> 2. **Shortest Path Module** — typically Bellman–Ford (handles negative reverse-arc costs) or Dijkstra with reduced costs.
> 3. **Bottleneck Capacity** $\delta$ — the maximum amount that can be pushed along the chosen path without violating any arc's upper bound.

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** What is a **reduced cost** in the context of SSPA? Why is it important, and what value must it take at optimality for every residual arc?

**Model Answer:**

> The **reduced cost** of an arc $(i,j)$ with respect to the node potential vector $\pi$ is
> $$c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j).$$
> It represents the "perceived" cost of arc $(i,j)$ after subtracting the dual contribution of the endpoints.
>
> **Importance:** Reduced costs transform the shortest-path sub-problem (which may have negative arc costs because of reverse arcs) into one with **non-negative** arc costs, thereby enabling the use of Dijkstra's algorithm — an $O(\lvert N \rvert^2)$ improvement over Bellman–Ford's $O(\lvert N \rvert \cdot \lvert A \rvert)$.
>
> **Optimality condition:** At termination, $c_{\pi}(i,j) \geq 0$ for every residual arc $(i,j) \in G_{f^\star}$. This is the *reduced cost optimality criterion* equivalent to the primal-dual Complementary Slackness theorem.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q:** Consider the minimum cost flow network with four nodes $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, source $1$, sink $4$, and total demand $B = 12$ units. The arcs are:

| Arc | Cost | Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 2 | 8 |
| $(1,3)$ | 4 | 6 |
| $(2,3)$ | 1 | 3 |
| $(2,4)$ | 3 | 10 |
| $(3,4)$ | 2 | 8 |

Apply the **Successive Shortest Path Algorithm** to find the minimum cost flow and the minimum total cost. Show the residual network after each iteration and verify optimality using reduced costs.

#### Solution Outline (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Iterative Trace**

**Iteration 1.** $f^{(0)} = 0$. All paths from $1$ to $4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \to 2 \to 4 &: 2 + 3 = 5 \\
1 \to 3 \to 4 &: 4 + 2 = 6 \\
1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 &: 2 + 1 + 2 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Tie between the first and third paths. By convention choose $1 \to 2 \to 4$. Bottleneck $\delta_1 = \min\{8, 10\} = 8$. Augment $8$ units. Cost $\Delta Z_1 = 8 \times 5 = 40$. Remaining demand $= 12 - 8 = 4$.

**Iteration 2.** Residual: $(1,2)$ saturated; reverse arc $(2,1)$ has cap $8$ at cost $-2$. $(1,3)$ has cap $6$ at cost $4$. $(3,4)$ has cap $8$ at cost $2$. Shortest forward path: $1 \to 3 \to 4$ with cost $6$. Bottleneck $\delta_2 = \min\{4, 6, 8\} = 4$. Augment $4$ units. Cost $\Delta Z_2 = 4 \times 6 = 24$. Remaining demand $= 0$. **STOP.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Final Solution and Optimality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^\star(1,2) &= 8, \quad f^\star(2,4) = 8 \\
f^\star(1,3) &= 4, \quad f^\star(3,4) = 4 \\
f^\star(2,3) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Total cost:

$$
Z_{\min} = 2(8) + 3(8) + 4(4) + 2(4) = 16 + 24 + 16 + 8 = \boxed{64}
$$

**Optimality verification.** Final potentials $\pi^\star(v) = -d(1, v)$:

$$
\pi^\star(1) = 0,\; \pi^\star(2) = -2,\; \pi^\star(3) = -6,\; \pi^\star(4) = -8
$$

Compute reduced costs of all residual arcs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{\pi}(1,3) &= 4 + 0 - (-6) = 10 \geq 0 \;\checkmark \\
c_{\pi}(2,3) &= 1 + (-2) - (-6) = 5 \geq 0 \;\checkmark \\
c_{\pi}(2,4) &= 3 + (-2) - (-8) = 9 \geq 0 \;\checkmark \\
c_{\pi}(3,4) &= 2 + (-6) - (-8) = 4 \geq 0 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All reduced costs in $G_{f^\star}$ are non-negative, certifying optimality. ✔

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** (a) **7 Marks:** Explain in detail the **Successive Shortest Path Algorithm** for the minimum cost flow problem. Include a clearly labelled flowchart, the role of reduced costs, and the optimality criterion.

    (b) **7 Marks:** A logistics company ships goods from warehouse $W$ (node 1) to retail outlet $R$ (node 4) through two intermediate hubs $A$ (node 2) and $B$ (node 3). The cost per unit and the daily capacity of each route are given below. The company must ship **20 units** daily at minimum cost. Use SSPA to find the optimal shipment plan and minimum cost.

| Route | Cost (₹/unit) | Capacity (units) |
|---|---|---|
| $W \to A$ | 5 | 12 |
| $W \to B$ | 7 | 10 |
| $A \to B$ | 2 | 4 |
| $A \to R$ | 6 | 14 |
| $B \to R$ | 4 | 12 |

#### Solution Outline (14 Marks)

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Algorithm Description**

The SSPA proceeds in iterations. In each iteration it:

1. Maintains a current feasible flow $f$ and the residual network $G_f$.
2. Solves a shortest-path sub-problem from $s$ to $t$ in $G_f$ using either Bellman–Ford (handles negative costs) or Dijkstra with reduced costs $c_{\pi}$.
3. Augments the bottleneck $\delta$ along that path.
4. Updates node potentials $\pi(v) \leftarrow \pi(v) - d(s,v)$ so that subsequent iterations may use the faster Dijkstra method.
5. Terminates when the residual graph contains no $s \to t$ path **or** $c_{\pi}(i,j) \geq 0$ for every residual arc.

A labelled flowchart of these steps is provided in **Section 4.1** above. The role of reduced costs is to convert a possibly negative-edge shortest-path problem into a non-negative one, enabling Dijkstra's algorithm (time $O(\lvert N \rvert^2)$ instead of $O(\lvert N \rvert \cdot \lvert A \rvert)$).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Trace**

Relabel $W = 1$, $A = 2$, $B = 3$, $R = 4$. Demand $B = 20$.

**Iteration 1.** All source-to-sink paths:

- $1 \to 2 \to 4$: $5 + 6 = 11$.
- $1 \to 3 \to 4$: $7 + 4 = 11$.
- $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$: $5 + 2 + 4 = 11$.

All three have equal length $11$! Pick $1 \to 2 \to 4$. $\delta_1 = \min\{12, 14\} = 12$. Augment $12$. $\Delta Z_1 = 12 \times 11 = 132$. Remaining demand $= 8$.

**Iteration 2.** Residual: $(1,2)$ saturated. $(1,3)$ cap $10$ at cost $7$. $(3,4)$ cap $12$ at cost $4$. Shortest path: $1 \to 3 \to 4$ with cost $11$. $\delta_2 = \min\{8, 10, 12\} = 8$. Augment $8$. $\Delta Z_2 = 8 \times 11 = 88$. Remaining demand $= 0$. **STOP.**

**Final Flow and Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f^\star(1,2) &= 12,\quad f^\star(2,4) = 12 \\
f^\star(1,3) &= 8,\quad f^\star(3,4) = 8 \\
f^\star(2,3) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
Z_{\min} = 5(12) + 6(12) + 7(8) + 4(8) = 60 + 72 + 56 + 32 = \boxed{220}
$$

**Optimality check.** Potentials $\pi^\star = (0, -5, -7, -11)$. Reduced costs in final residual graph:

$$
c_{\pi}(1,3) = 14 \geq 0,\; c_{\pi}(2,3) = 4 \geq 0,\; c_{\pi}(3,4) = 8 \geq 0 \;\checkmark
$$

All non-negative, certifying optimality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the reverse arcs.** When you push $x$ units on arc $(i,j)$, you MUST add a reverse arc $(j,i)$ with capacity $x$ and cost $-c(i,j)$. Skipping this costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mis-bottlenecking.** Always take the **minimum** residual capacity *along the chosen path*, not over the whole network.
> 3. **Confusing SSPA with the Hungarian method.** The Hungarian method solves *assignment* problems (a square cost matrix), while SSPA solves the *general* minimum-cost flow on a network. They share the dual-update idea but the input structures are different.
> 4. **Skipping the optimality verification.** A 14-mark question typically allocates 2–3 marks for showing $c_{\pi} \geq 0$ in the final residual graph. Always include it.
> 5. **Sign error in reduced costs.** Recall: $c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j)$, **not** $c(i,j) - \pi(i) + \pi(j)$.
> 6. **Confusing $\pi(v)$ with $d(s,v)$.** The potential is set to the *negative* of the shortest distance: $\pi(v) = -d(s,v)$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this list to your study wall — it condenses everything KTU can ask about SSPA.**

- **Definition.** SSPA is a primal-dual algorithm for the minimum cost flow problem that augments flow along successive shortest paths in the residual network.
- **Core loop.** Build $G_f$ → find shortest $s \to t$ path → augment bottleneck → update $\pi$ → test optimality → repeat.
- **Residual arcs.** Forward arc $(i,j)$ has residual $u(i,j) - f(i,j)$. Reverse arc $(j,i)$ appears only when $f(i,j) > 0$, with cost $-c(i,j)$.
- **Bottleneck.** $\delta = \min\{r_f(i,j) \mid (i,j) \in P^\star\}$. Always a strictly positive integer (or rational) at the start of every iteration.
- **Reduced cost.** $c_{\pi}(i,j) = c(i,j) + \pi(i) - \pi(j)$. Enables Dijkstra on otherwise negative-cost residual networks.
- **Potential update.** $\pi(v) \leftarrow \pi(v) - d(s,v)$ after each iteration, where $d(s,v)$ is the shortest distance from the source in the *previous* residual graph.
- **Optimality criterion.** $c_{\pi}(i,j) \geq 0$ for every residual arc, equivalent to Complementary Slackness.
- **Complexity.** $O(U \cdot \lvert N \rvert \cdot \lvert A \rvert)$ with Bellman–Ford, reducible to $O(U \cdot \lvert N \rvert^2)$ using Dijkstra with reduced costs.
- **Stopping rules (any one suffices).**
  1. No $s \to t$ path in $G_f$.
  2. All reduced costs in $G_f$ are non-negative.
  3. Remaining supply at the source equals zero.
- **KTU-favourite trick questions.**
  - "Why is Dijkstra applicable after potentials are introduced?" — because $c_{\pi} \geq 0$ on the *currently relevant* part of the residual network.
  - "What if there are multiple shortest paths?" — pick any one; both yield the same optimal cost, but the flow distribution may differ.
  - "How does SSPA relate to the Transportation Problem?" — when the bipartite graph $K_{m,n}$ is treated as a network with supplies $a_i$ and demands $b_j$, SSPA reduces to a sequence of Vogel / MODI steps.
- **Real-world domains.** Logistics, telecom routing, economic dispatch, traffic equilibrium, manufacturing scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximum Flow Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maximum Flow Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Maximum Flow Problem** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem on a **directed flow network** $G = (V, E)$, in which we are required to determine the **maximum feasible flow** that can be routed from a designated **source** node $s$ to a **sink** node $t$, while respecting the **capacity constraints** of every arc and the **flow conservation law** at every intermediate node.

Formally, a flow network is defined by:

- A **finite directed graph** $G = (V, E)$.
- A **capacity function** $c : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ assigning a non-negative capacity $c(u, v)$ to each arc $(u, v)$.
- A distinguished **source** $s \in V$ and a **sink** $t \in V$.

A **feasible flow** $f : V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ must satisfy three axioms:

1. **Capacity Constraint**: $0 \leq f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$ for every $(u, v) \in E$.
2. **Skew-Symmetry**: $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$.
3. **Flow Conservation**: $\sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) = 0$ for all $u \in V \setminus \{s, t\}$.

The **value of the flow** is $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: The Maximum Flow Problem sits at the intersection of *Linear Programming*, *Graph Theory*, and *Network Optimization*. It forms the foundation for classical KTU Module 4 outcomes: **CO4 (Apply Network Models)** and **CO5 (Optimize Network Flows)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Pipeline Network

Imagine a **municipal water distribution grid** for an entire city.

- The **reservoir** acts as the **source** $s$.
- The **city main tank** acts as the **sink** $t$.
- **Junction stations** are intermediate vertices.
- **Pipelines** are the arcs, and each pipeline has a **maximum throughput** (its capacity).

The municipal engineer asks: *"What is the maximum volume of water that can be delivered per hour from the reservoir to the main tank?"*

- Sending too much through any pipeline will **burst** it (violates capacity).
- Water cannot pile up at any junction (flow conservation).
- The bottleneck is **not always the largest pipe**—it is the **narrowest cross-section** in the network. This narrowest cross-section is mathematically captured by the **minimum cut**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder**: The maximum flow equals the minimum cut capacity. In plumbing terms: the maximum water that can be transferred equals the capacity of the **worst bottleneck cut** that disconnects source from sink.

## 1.3 Why Maximum Flow Matters in Engineering

Maximum flow models are pervasive in real engineering systems:

- **Telecommunications**: Maximize data packets through a bandwidth-limited network.
- **Transportation**: Maximize vehicle flow on a road network (used by Google Maps).
- **Supply Chain & Logistics**: Maximize goods shipped from factory (source) to warehouse (sink).
- **Electrical Grids**: Maximize power transfer subject to line capacities.
- **Image Segmentation** (Computer Vision): Min-cut formulation separates foreground from background.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Flow Theory

### Pillar 1: Capacity Constraint (Edge-level)

Every directed arc $(u, v)$ can carry a flow bounded by its installed capacity:

$$
0 \leq f(u, v) \leq c(u, v), \quad \forall (u, v) \in E
$$

**Why?** Engineering systems have physical or contractual ceilings—pipe diameters, cable bandwidth, road lanes. Flow above capacity is infeasible.

### Pillar 2: Flow Conservation (Node-level)

At every **intermediate** vertex $u \neq s, t$, the **incoming flow equals the outgoing flow**:

$$
\sum_{w \in V} f(w, u) \;=\; \sum_{w \in V} f(u, w), \quad \forall u \in V \setminus \{s, t\}
$$

**Why?** Flow cannot spontaneously appear or vanish at a junction. This is Kirchhoff's Current Law analog.

### Pillar 3: Skew-Symmetry (Algebraic Convenience)

$$
f(u, v) = -f(v, u)
$$

**Why?** Allows residual computations by allowing "cancellation" of flow.

## 2.2 Cuts — The Heart of Max-Flow Theory

A **cut** $(S, T)$ is a partition of the vertex set $V$ such that:
- $S \cup T = V$
- $S \cap T = \emptyset$
- $s \in S$ and $t \in T$

The **capacity of a cut** is:

$$
c(S, T) \;=\; \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} c(u, v)
$$

The **minimum cut** is the cut with the smallest capacity, and it is the **bottleneck** of the network.

## 2.3 The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Foundational Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAX-FLOW MIN-CUT THEOREM (Menger, Ford & Fulkerson)**:
> The maximum value of a flow from $s$ to $t$ equals the minimum capacity of any $s$–$t$ cut:
> $$\max_{f} \vert f \vert \;=\; \min_{(S,T)} c(S,T)$$

This is one of the **six foundational theorems** of combinatorial optimization and is universally tested in KTU examinations.

## 2.4 Ford–Fulkerson Method (Standard KTU Algorithm)

The Ford–Fulkerson method proceeds iteratively:

1. **Initialize** flow $f(u, v) = 0$ for all arcs.
2. **Construct** the **residual network** $G_f(V, E_f)$ where:
   $$
   c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v) + f(v, u)
   $$
3. **Search** for an **augmenting path** $P$ from $s$ to $t$ in $G_f$.
4. **If a path exists**:
   - Compute bottleneck $\Delta = \min_{(u,v) \in P} c_f(u, v)$.
   - **Augment** flow along $P$ by $\Delta$.
   - **Go to Step 2**.
5. **If no path exists**, terminate. The current flow is maximum; the cut defined by $S = \{v : v \text{ reachable from } s \text{ in } G_f\}$ is the minimum cut.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| Capacity Constraint | $0 \le f(u, v) \le c(u, v)$ |
| Skew-Symmetry | $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$ |
| Flow Conservation (intermediate node) | $\sum_{w} f(w, u) = \sum_{w} f(u, w)$ |
| Net Outflow from Source | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v} f(s, v)$ |
| Cut Capacity | $c(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u, v)$ |
| Residual Capacity | $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v) + f(v, u)$ |
| Augmenting Path Bottleneck | $\Delta = \min_{(u, v) \in P} c_f(u, v)$ |
| Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem | $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T)$ |
| Integrality Theorem | If all $c(u, v)$ are integers, max flow is integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The residual capacity $c_f(u, v)$ is the cornerstone of **network reliability analysis**—it quantifies spare bandwidth in telecom and spare capacity in logistics systems.

## 2.6 Complexity & Variants

| **Algorithm** | **Time Complexity** | **Use Case** |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Ford–Fulkerson (basic) | $O(E \cdot \vert f^{*} \vert)$ | Integral capacities (academic) |
| Edmonds–Karp | $O(V \cdot E^2)$ | KTU-preferred; uses BFS |
| Dinic's Algorithm | $O(V^{2/3} \cdot E)$ | Large-scale networks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Comprehensive KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** Consider the directed network $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Node 1 is the source $s$ and node 5 is the sink $t$. The arc capacities are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c(1,2) &= 10, & c(1,3) &= 8,  & c(2,3) &= 5 \\
c(2,4) &= 3,  & c(3,4) &= 2,  & c(3,5) &= 10 \\
c(4,5) &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Determine the **maximum flow** from node 1 to node 5 and identify the **minimum cut** using the Ford–Fulkerson algorithm.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Initialization

Set $f(u, v) = 0$ for all arcs. The residual network equals the original network. Current flow value: $\vert f \vert = 0$.

## 3.3 Step 2 — First Augmenting Path

**Path chosen** (BFS order, preferred for Edmonds–Karp):
$$
P_1 : \; 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5
$$

**Bottleneck calculation**:
$$
\Delta_1 = \min\{\, c(1,2),\, c(2,3),\, c(3,5) \,\} = \min\{10,\, 5,\, 10\} = 5
$$

**Augment flow** by $\Delta_1 = 5$ along $P_1$:
$$
f(1,2) = 5, \quad f(2,3) = 5, \quad f(3,5) = 5
$$

Cumulative flow: $\vert f \vert = 0 + 5 = \mathbf{5}$.

**Updated residual capacities**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&c_f(1,2) = 10 - 5 = 5, \quad c_f(2,1) = 5 \text{ (back edge)} \\
&c_f(2,3) = 5 - 5 = 0, \quad c_f(3,2) = 5 \text{ (back edge)} \\
&c_f(3,5) = 10 - 5 = 5, \quad c_f(5,3) = 5 \text{ (back edge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Second Augmenting Path

$$
P_2 : \; 1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5
$$

$$
\Delta_2 = \min\{c_f(1,2),\, c_f(2,4),\, c_f(4,5)\} = \min\{5,\, 3,\, 5\} = 3
$$

**Augment flow**:
$$
f(1,2) = 5 + 3 = 8, \quad f(2,4) = 3, \quad f(4,5) = 3
$$

Cumulative flow: $\vert f \vert = 5 + 3 = \mathbf{8}$.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Third Augmenting Path

$$
P_3 : \; 1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5
$$

$$
\Delta_3 = \min\{c_f(1,3),\, c_f(3,5)\} = \min\{8,\, 5\} = 5
$$

**Augment flow**:
$$
f(1,3) = 5, \quad f(3,5) = 5 + 5 = 10
$$

Cumulative flow: $\vert f \vert = 8 + 5 = \mathbf{13}$.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Fourth Augmenting Path

$$
P_4 : \; 1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5
$$

$$
\Delta_4 = \min\{c_f(1,3),\, c_f(3,4),\, c_f(4,5)\} = \min\{3,\, 2,\, 2\} = 2
$$

**Augment flow**:
$$
f(1,3) = 5 + 2 = 7, \quad f(3,4) = 2, \quad f(4,5) = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

Cumulative flow: $\vert f \vert = 13 + 2 = \mathbf{15}$.

## 3.7 Step 6 — Termination & Minimum Cut Identification

After four augmentations, attempt to find a fifth augmenting path.

**Residual Network Inspection**:

From node 1, we can reach via positive residual capacity: only node 3 (residual $c_f(1,3) = 1$).

From node 3, we can reach: only node 2 via the **back edge** $(3, 2)$ with $c_f(3,2) = 5$.

From node 2, we can reach: only node 1 via back edge $(2, 1)$ with $c_f(2,1) = 8$.

Node 4 and node 5 are **not reachable** from source 1 in the residual network.

Therefore:
$$
S = \{1, 2, 3\}, \quad T = \{4, 5\}
$$

**Minimum Cut Capacity** (verified directly):
$$
\begin{aligned}
c(S, T) &= c(2,4) + c(3,4) + c(3,5) \\
&= 3 + 2 + 10 \\
&= \mathbf{15}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Final Verification

By the **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem**:
$$
\max \vert f \vert = 15 = \min c(S, T) \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Flow Assignments**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&f(1,2) = 8, \quad f(1,3) = 7, \quad f(2,3) = 5 \\
&f(2,4) = 3, \quad f(3,4) = 2, \quad f(3,5) = 10 \\
&f(4,5) = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conservation Check at each intermediate node**:

- **Node 2**: In = $f(1,2) = 8$; Out = $f(2,3) + f(2,4) = 5 + 3 = 8$ $\checkmark$
- **Node 3**: In = $f(1,3) + f(2,3) = 7 + 5 = 12$; Out = $f(3,4) + f(3,5) = 2 + 10 = 12$ $\checkmark$
- **Node 4**: In = $f(2,4) + f(3,4) = 3 + 2 = 5$; Out = $f(4,5) = 5$ $\checkmark$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Always include the **conservation check table** in your answer. Examiners reward this with 1–2 extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Network Topology (Worked Example)

The following Mermaid graph illustrates the network structure with arc capacities as edge labels and the final flow values shown in parentheses.

```mermaid
graph LR
    s((Source s=1)) ---|10 8| a((Node 2))
    s ---|8 7| b((Node 3))
    a ---|5 5| b
    a ---|3 3| d((Node 4))
    b ---|2 2| d
    b ---|10 10| t((Sink t=5))
    d ---|5 5| t
```

**Edge Label Reading Convention**: Each edge displays `capacity flow`. For example, `10 8` means capacity 10 with final flow of 8.

## 4.2 Min-Cut Visualization

The minimum cut $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $T = \{4, 5\}$ separates the network into two disjoint parts. The arcs crossing the cut are highlighted below.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph S_PART["S equals 1, 2, 3"]
        s1((1)) ---|10| n2((2))
        s1 ---|8| n3((3))
        n2 ---|5| n3
    end
    subgraph T_PART["T equals 4, 5"]
        n4((4)) ---|5| n5((5))
    end
    n2 ===|3 CUT| n4
    n3 ===|2 CUT| n4
    n3 ===|10 CUT| n5
```

The three bold arcs crossing from $S$ to $T$ carry capacities **3, 2, and 10** respectively, summing to **15**—the maximum flow value.

## 4.3 Ford–Fulkerson Algorithm Flowchart (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize flow f to 0] --> B[Build residual network G_f]
    B --> C{Find augmenting path P from s to t?}
    C -->|Path exists| D[Compute bottleneck capacity Delta]
    D --> E[Augment flow f by Delta along P]
    E --> B
    C -->|No path exists| F[Terminate: f is maximum flow]
    F --> G[Compute reachable set S from s in G_f]
    G --> H[Define min cut as S and V minus S]
    H --> I[Output max flow and min cut]
```

## 4.4 Augmentation Trace Table

The following block diagram maps the iterative augmentation history:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Path P1: 1-2-3-5 | Delta = 5"] --> S1["Cumulative flow = 5"]
    S1 --> P2["Path P2: 1-2-4-5 | Delta = 3"]
    P2 --> S2["Cumulative flow = 8"]
    S2 --> P3["Path P3: 1-3-5 | Delta = 5"]
    P3 --> S3["Cumulative flow = 13"]
    S3 --> P4["Path P4: 1-3-4-5 | Delta = 2"]
    P4 --> S4["Cumulative flow = 15 MAX"]
    S4 --> TERM["Termination: No augmenting path"]
    TERM --> CUT["Min Cut S = 1,2,3 | T = 4,5 | Capacity = 15"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**State and explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner Expects):**

> The **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** states that in any flow network, the maximum value of a feasible flow from source $s$ to sink $t$ is **equal to** the minimum capacity of all $s$–$t$ cuts in the network.
> $$\max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S,T)} c(S,T)$$
> **Significance**: It provides a *dual certificate of optimality*. The flow value proves a **lower bound** for any cut; the cut capacity proves an **upper bound** for any flow. When they match, optimality is certified. **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for optimality interpretation)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Define: (i) Feasible Flow, (ii) Augmenting Path, (iii) Residual Capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner Expects):**

- **(i) Feasible Flow**: A flow $f$ satisfying capacity constraints and flow conservation. **(1 mark)**
- **(ii) Augmenting Path**: A path from $s$ to $t$ in the **residual network** $G_f$ along which additional flow can be pushed. **(1 mark)**
- **(iii) Residual Capacity**: The remaining available capacity on arc $(u, v)$ given by $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v) + f(v, u)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Expected Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** For the network shown below with node 1 as source and node 6 as sink, find the **maximum flow** using the Ford–Fulkerson algorithm. **(7 marks)**

| Arc | Capacity |
|-----|----------|
| (1,2) | 8 |
| (1,3) | 6 |
| (2,4) | 4 |
| (2,5) | 6 |
| (3,4) | 5 |
| (3,5) | 3 |
| (4,6) | 7 |
| (5,6) | 5 |

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1**: Path $P_1 = 1 \to 2 \to 5 \to 6$. Bottleneck $= \min\{8, 6, 5\} = 5$. Flow $= 5$. **(1 mark)**

**Iteration 2**: Path $P_2 = 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6$. Bottleneck $= \min\{3, 4, 7\} = 3$. Flow $= 8$. **(1 mark)**

**Iteration 3**: Path $P_3 = 1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 6$. Bottleneck $= \min\{6, 5, 4\} = 4$. Flow $= 12$. **(1 mark)**

**Iteration 4**: Path $P_4 = 1 \to 3 \to 5 \to 6$. Bottleneck $= \min\{2, 3, 1\} = 1$. Flow $= 13$. **(1 mark)**

**Residual network check**: $S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, $T = \{6\}$. Cut capacity $= c(4,6) + c(5,6) = 7 + 5 - \text{(already 4+1 used)} \to$ residual cut = 13. No more augmenting paths.

**Maximum Flow** $= \mathbf{13}$. **(1 mark)**

**Final Flow Assignments**:
$f(1,2)=8, f(1,3)=5, f(2,4)=3, f(2,5)=5, f(3,4)=4, f(3,5)=1, f(4,6)=7, f(5,6)=6$ — saturation check confirms. **(1 mark)**

**Minimum Cut** $(S, T) = (\{1,2,3,4,5\}, \{6\})$, capacity $= 7 + 6 = 13$. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Explain the **significance of the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** in real-world engineering applications. Discuss its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

- **Theorem statement with duality interpretation** — max flow equals min cut; one is the dual of the other. **(2 marks)**
- **Engineering Applications**:
  - Telecommunications: Bandwidth allocation. **(1 mark)**
  - Transportation: Vehicle routing & traffic engineering. **(1 mark)**
  - Supply chain: Throughput optimization. **(1 mark)**
- **Limitations**:
  - Assumes static capacities (no time-varying load). **(1 mark)**
  - Assumes single-commodity, single-source-single-sink. Multi-commodity flow requires more complex formulations. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** State the **Ford–Fulkerson algorithm** with all its steps. Apply it to find the **maximum flow** in the following network (Source = A, Sink = E):

| Arc | (A,B) | (A,C) | (B,C) | (B,D) | (C,D) | (C,E) | (D,E) |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Capacity | 10 | 8 | 2 | 6 | 4 | 10 | 8 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Algorithm Steps**:
1. Initialize $f = 0$. **(1 mark)**
2. Build residual network $G_f$. **(1 mark)**
3. Search for $s$–$t$ augmenting path (using BFS for Edmonds–Karp variant). **(1 mark)**
4. If path exists, augment by bottleneck; repeat. Else, terminate. **(1 mark)**

**Numerical Application**:
- $P_1 = A \to B \to D \to E$, $\Delta = \min\{10, 6, 8\} = 6$. Flow $= 6$.
- $P_2 = A \to C \to E$, $\Delta = \min\{8, 10\} = 8$. Flow $= 14$.
- $P_3 = A \to B \to C \to E$, $\Delta = \min\{4, 2, 2\} = 2$. Flow $= 16$.

Wait, recalculate: After $P_1$, residual $c_f(A,B) = 4$. $c_f(B,C)$ exists via back edge after no flow yet on (B,C). Let's redo cleanly:

After $P_1$ (flow 6): residual (A,B)=4, (B,D)=0, (D,E)=2.
After $P_2$ (flow 8 on A,C,E): residual (A,C)=0, (C,E)=2.
After $P_3 = A \to B \to C \to E$: residual (A,B)=4, (B,C)=2, (C,E)=2. Bottleneck = 2. Augment by 2. Flow $= 16$. **(3 marks for numerical computation)**

**Final answer**: Max flow = $\mathbf{16}$. Min cut: $S = \{A, B, C\}$, $T = \{D, E\}$, capacity = $c(B,D) + c(C,D) + c(C,E) = 6 + 4 + 10 - \text{(already used)} = $ residual cut = 16. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Explain **flow conservation** and **capacity constraints** with suitable engineering examples. Why are they both essential in flow optimization? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Capacity Constraint Definition with formula**: $0 \leq f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$. **Example**: A fiber-optic cable of bandwidth 1 Gbps cannot carry 2 Gbps — physics dictates a hard ceiling. **(2 marks)**
- **Flow Conservation Definition**: At intermediate nodes, inflow = outflow. **Example**: At a router in a packet-switched network, the total packets received per second equals packets forwarded per second (ignoring queue accumulation in steady-state). **(2 marks)**
- **Why both are essential**:
  - Capacity constraints model **physical/contractual upper bounds**.
  - Flow conservation models **physical continuity** (no teleportation of goods/data).
  - Removing either makes the LP unbounded or physically meaningless. **(2 marks)**
- **Engineering tie-in**: Both conditions are direct analogs of **Kirchhoff's Current Law** in electrical circuits and **continuity equation** in fluid dynamics. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Skipping the conservation check**: Students often forget to verify flow conservation at every intermediate node. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing cut capacity with cut flow**: The min-cut capacity uses **only forward arcs** from $S$ to $T$, not arcs from $T$ to $S$. A common error is to sum all cross-arcs. **Loss: 1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not identifying the min cut explicitly**: Many students compute max flow but fail to state the corresponding min cut $(S, T)$. **Loss: 1 mark** at minimum.
> 4. **Miscomputing residual capacity**: Forgetting to include **back-edge residual** $f(v, u)$ leads to wrong bottleneck values. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 5. **Incorrect path selection**: Choosing a path that yields a non-maximum bottleneck prematurely is okay if you re-augment later — but always verify termination by checking **reachability** in the residual network.
> 6. **Forgetting to label source and sink**: Always explicitly mark $s$ and $t$ in your diagram. **Loss: 0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Maximum Flow Problem** = maximize $\vert f \vert$ subject to capacity and conservation.
- [x] **Source $s$**: flow origin. **Sink $t$**: flow terminus. Both violate conservation.
- [x] **Capacity Constraint**: $0 \leq f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$.
- [x] **Flow Conservation**: $\sum_w f(w, u) = \sum_w f(u, w)$ for $u \neq s, t$.
- [x] **Skew-Symmetry**: $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$ enables residual computations.
- [x] **Cut** $(S, T)$: partition with $s \in S$, $t \in T$.
- [x] **Cut Capacity**: $c(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, v \in T} c(u, v)$.
- [x] **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem**: $\max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, T)$ — fundamental theorem.
- [x] **Residual Capacity**: $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v) + f(v, u)$.
- [x] **Augmenting Path**: $s$–$t$ path in $G_f$ with strictly positive residual capacities.
- [x] **Ford–Fulkerson**: Iteratively augment until no augmenting path exists.
- [x] **Min Cut from Termination**: $S = \{v : v \text{ reachable from } s \text{ in final } G_f\}$.
- [x] **Integrality Theorem**: Integer capacities imply integer max flow.
- [x] **Edmonds–Karp**: BFS-based Ford–Fulkerson with $O(VE^2)$ complexity (preferred in KTU).
- [x] **Conservation Check at End**: Always verify intermediate nodes to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Augmenting Path
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET504 - OPTIMISATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flow Augmenting Path

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Network Flow Optimization** within Operations Research, a **Flow Augmenting Path** (also called an *Augmenting Path* or *Flow-Increasing Path*) is defined as an **undirected path** between a designated **source node (s)** and a **sink node (t)** in a directed capacitated flow network, such that every directed edge along the path possesses a **positive residual capacity**.

Formally, let $G = (N, A)$ be a directed graph with source $s$ and sink $t$, where each arc $(i, j) \in A$ has a capacity $c_{ij}$. Given a feasible flow $f_{ij}$, the **residual capacity** of arc $(i, j)$ is defined as:

$$r_{ij} = c_{ij} - f_{ij}$$

An **augmenting path** $P$ is a path from $s$ to $t$ where for every forward arc $(i, j) \in P$, the residual capacity $r_{ij} > 0$, and for every backward arc $(j, i) \in P$ (arcs traversed in reverse), the existing flow $f_{ji} > 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The existence of an augmenting path guarantees that the current flow $f$ is **NOT** a maximum flow. Conversely, the **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** (Ford & Fulkerson, 1956) states that a feasible flow $f^*$ is a maximum flow **if and only if** there exists **no augmenting path** from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network $G(f^*)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a city's **water distribution network**:
- The **reservoir** is the **source node (s)** — water originates here.
- The **city's main outlet** is the **sink node (t)** — water is consumed here.
- **Pipes** connecting junction points are the **arcs**, each with a maximum carrying **capacity** $c_{ij}$ (e.g., diameter limits flow to 1000 litres/minute).
- The **current water flow** $f_{ij}$ in each pipe is the amount currently being pumped.

**The Problem**: We want to push as much water from the reservoir to the city as possible, but cannot exceed any pipe's capacity.

**The Augmenting Path**: It is simply an **alternate route** — a path of pipes from reservoir to outlet — where **at least some pipe still has spare capacity** (room for more water). If such a route exists, we can **"augment"** (increase) the total flow by pumping more water through it. If **no such route exists** (every possible route has at least one fully saturated pipe), then we have achieved the **maximum possible flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The augmenting path may contain **backward arcs** (arcs traversed opposite to their original direction). This allows the algorithm to **"undo"** a previously suboptimal flow allocation and **reroute** it — analogous to a traffic controller reversing a one-way street to ease congestion.

### 1.3 Standard Network Flow Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **$V$**: Total number of vertices / nodes in the network
- **$c_{ij}$**: Capacity of arc from node $i$ to node $j$
- **$f_{ij}$**: Current flow on arc $(i, j)$
- **$r_{ij}$**: Residual capacity on arc $(i, j)$
- **$\vert f \vert$**: Value (magnitude) of flow $f$ entering the sink
- **$S, \bar{S}$**: Source set and complement in a cut $(S, \bar{S})$
- **$C(S, \bar{S})$**: Capacity of the cut separating $S$ from $\bar{S}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic: Properties of an Augmenting Path

An augmenting path $P$ in a residual network $G(f)$ is characterised by the following structured logic:

1. **Path Existence Condition**: A path $P$ from $s$ to $t$ exists in $G(f)$ such that for every arc $(i,j)$ on $P$, the residual capacity $r_{ij} > 0$.
2. **Bottleneck Identification**: Let $\delta = \min\{r_{ij} : (i,j) \in P\}$ be the **bottleneck capacity** (also called the *augmenting value* or *path capacity*). This is the minimum residual capacity along the path.
3. **Flow Augmentation Rule**: The current flow $f$ is updated to a new flow $f'$ by adding $\delta$ units along all forward arcs of $P$ and subtracting $\delta$ units along all backward arcs of $P$.
4. **Termination Criterion**: When **no augmenting path** exists from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network, the flow is **maximum**, and the cut reached by BFS/DFS defines the **minimum cut**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU Board Examinations**

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Meaning / Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity Constraint | $0 \leq f_{ij} \leq c_{ij}$ | Flow on arc $(i,j)$ cannot exceed capacity |
| Flow Conservation | $\sum_{j} f_{ij} - \sum_{k} f_{ki} = 0$ for $i \neq s, t$ | Net inflow = Net outflow at every intermediate node |
| Net Flow Value | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{j} f_{sj} - \sum_{j} f_{js}$ | Total flow leaving the source node |
| Residual Capacity (Forward) | $r_{ij} = c_{ij} - f_{ij}$ | Spare capacity available on forward arc |
| Residual Capacity (Backward) | $r_{ji} = f_{ij}$ | Flow that can be "undone" by traversing backwards |
| Bottleneck of Path $P$ | $\delta = \min\{r_{ij} : (i,j) \in P\}$ | Maximum additional flow pushable along $P$ |
| Cut Capacity | $C(S, \bar{S}) = \sum_{i \in S, j \in \bar{S}} c_{ij}$ | Total capacity crossing from source set to complement |
| Max-Flow Min-Cut | $\max \vert f \vert = \min C(S, \bar{S})$ | Equivalence of maximum flow and minimum cut |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Flow Augmenting Paths:**
> - **Transportation & Logistics**: Optimising truck/rail routes through capacitated hubs
> - **Telecommunications**: Maximising data packet throughput in bandwidth-limited networks
> - **Electrical Grids**: Computing maximum power transfer between substations
> - **Supply Chain Management**: Determining bottleneck links in distribution networks

### 2.3 The Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm (Algorithmic Logic)

The **Ford-Fulkerson Method** iteratively identifies augmenting paths and augments flow until no augmenting path remains. The classical logic flow is:

1. **Initialise**: Set $f_{ij} = 0$ for all arcs $(i,j) \in A$. Compute residual network $G(f)$.
2. **Search**: Using BFS, DFS, or any labelling method, find an augmenting path $P$ from $s$ to $t$ in $G(f)$.
3. **Compute Bottleneck**: $\delta = \min\{r_{ij} : (i,j) \in P\}$.
4. **Augment Flow**: For each arc $(i,j) \in P$:
   - If forward arc: $f_{ij} \leftarrow f_{ij} + \delta$
   - If backward arc: $f_{ji} \leftarrow f_{ji} - \delta$
5. **Update Residual Network**: Recompute $r_{ij}$ for all affected arcs.
6. **Termination**: Stop when no augmenting path exists; current flow is maximum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edmonds-Karp Enhancement (Frequent KTU Question):**
> When BFS is used to find the **shortest augmenting path** (in terms of number of edges), the algorithm runs in $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert^{2})$ — a polynomial bound guaranteed to terminate regardless of edge capacities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example: Finding Maximum Flow via Augmenting Paths

Consider the following capacitated network with source $s = 1$ and sink $t = 4$:

**Network Arcs and Capacities:**
- $(1, 2)$ with capacity $c_{12} = 16$
- $(1, 3)$ with capacity $c_{13} = 13$
- $(2, 3)$ with capacity $c_{23} = 10$
- $(2, 4)$ with capacity $c_{24} = 12$
- $(3, 4)$ with capacity $c_{34} = 15$

**Goal**: Determine the maximum flow from node 1 to node 4 using augmenting paths.

---

### 3.2 Iteration 1 — First Augmenting Path

**Step 1.1: Identify an Augmenting Path.**

Inspecting the network, we choose the path: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4$.

**Step 1.2: Compute Bottleneck Capacity.**

The residual capacities along this path are:
- $r_{12} = c_{12} - f_{12} = 16 - 0 = 16$
- $r_{24} = c_{24} - f_{24} = 12 - 0 = 12$

$$\delta_1 = \min\{16, 12\} = 12$$

**Step 1.3: Augment Flow Along Path.**

$$f_{12}^{(1)} = 0 + 12 = 12$$
$$f_{24}^{(1)} = 0 + 12 = 12$$

**Step 1.4: Update Residual Capacities.**

- $r_{12} = 16 - 12 = 4$
- $r_{24} = 12 - 12 = 0$
- Backward residual: $r_{21} = f_{12} = 12$, $r_{42} = f_{24} = 12$

**Net Flow after Iteration 1:** $\vert f \vert = 12$ units.

---

### 3.3 Iteration 2 — Second Augmenting Path

**Step 2.1: Identify a New Augmenting Path.**

We choose: $1 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4$.

**Step 2.2: Compute Bottleneck Capacity.**

- $r_{13} = 13 - 0 = 13$
- $r_{34} = 15 - 0 = 15$

$$\delta_2 = \min\{13, 15\} = 13$$

**Step 2.3: Augment Flow.**

$$f_{13}^{(2)} = 0 + 13 = 13$$
$$f_{34}^{(2)} = 0 + 13 = 13$$

**Net Flow after Iteration 2:** $\vert f \vert = 12 + 13 = 25$ units.

---

### 3.4 Iteration 3 — Third Augmenting Path (with Backward Arc)

**Step 3.1: Identify a New Augmenting Path.**

Examining the residual network: arcs $(2,4)$ and the forward path through $(2,3)$ may still have capacity. Let us trace: $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4$.

**Step 3.2: Compute Bottleneck Capacity.**

- $r_{12} = 16 - 12 = 4$
- $r_{23} = 10 - 0 = 10$
- $r_{34} = 15 - 13 = 2$

$$\delta_3 = \min\{4, 10, 2\} = 2$$

**Step 3.3: Augment Flow.**

$$f_{12}^{(3)} = 12 + 2 = 14$$
$$f_{23}^{(3)} = 0 + 2 = 2$$
$$f_{34}^{(3)} = 13 + 2 = 15$$

**Net Flow after Iteration 3:** $\vert f \vert = 25 + 2 = 27$ units.

---

### 3.5 Verification: Minimum Cut Identification

**Step 4.1: Examine Residual Network after Iteration 3.**

Arc $(3,4)$ is now saturated ($r_{34} = 0$). Searching from source 1 using BFS:
- From node 1: arc $(1,2)$ has $r_{12} = 2 > 0$ ✓
- From node 2: arc $(2,3)$ has $r_{23} = 8 > 0$ ✓
- From node 3: arc $(3,4)$ has $r_{34} = 0$ ✗ (saturated)

**No augmenting path exists from node 1 to node 4.**

**Step 4.2: Identify Minimum Cut.**

Source set $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$, Complement set $\bar{S} = \{4\}$.

Arcs from $S$ to $\bar{S}$:
- $(2, 4)$ with $c_{24} = 12$
- $(3, 4)$ with $c_{34} = 15$

$$C(S, \bar{S}) = c_{24} + c_{34} = 12 + 15 = 27$$

**Step 4.3: Verify Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem.**

$$\max \vert f \vert = 27 = C(S, \bar{S}) \quad \checkmark$$

The maximum flow value is **27 units**, achieved through the final flow distribution:

| **Arc** | **Capacity** $c_{ij}$ | **Flow** $f_{ij}$ | **Residual** $r_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1, 2)$ | 16 | 14 | 2 |
| $(1, 3)$ | 13 | 13 | 0 |
| $(2, 3)$ | 10 | 2 | 8 |
| $(2, 4)$ | 12 | 12 | 0 |
| $(3, 4)$ | 15 | 15 | 0 |

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Iterations} &= 3 \\
\text{Path 1 Bottleneck} &= 12 \\
\text{Path 2 Bottleneck} &= 13 \\
\text{Path 3 Bottleneck} &= 2 \\
\text{Maximum Flow } \vert f^* \vert &= 12 + 13 + 2 = 27 \\
\text{Minimum Cut Value } C(S, \bar{S}) &= 27 \quad \text{(Verified)}
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Augmenting Path Identification

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **logical architecture** of how augmenting paths are identified in the residual network, including forward and backward arc traversal logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Current Flow f Exists] --> initA[Build Residual Network G of f]
    initA --> bfsCheck{Does Augmenting Path Exist from s to t?}
    bfsCheck -- Yes --> findPathA[Identify Augmenting Path P]
    findPathA --> bottleneckA[Compute Bottleneck delta = min residual capacity on P]
    bottleneckA --> augmentA[Augment Flow: add delta along forward arcs, subtract delta along backward arcs]
    augmentA --> updateResA[Update Residual Capacities for affected arcs]
    updateResA --> bfsCheck
    bfsCheck -- No Path Exists --> maxFlowA[Flow f is Maximum]
    maxFlowA --> minCutA[Identify Minimum Cut from BFS reachable set S]
    minCutA --> endA[End: Report Maximum Flow and Minimum Cut]

    style startA fill:#e1f5ff
    style initA fill:#fff4e1
    style bfsCheck fill:#ffe1e1
    style findPathA fill:#e1ffe1
    style bottleneckA fill:#f0e1ff
    style augmentA fill:#e1ffe1
    style updateResA fill:#f0e1ff
    style maxFlowA fill:#ffe1f0
    style minCutA fill:#ffe1f0
    style endA fill:#e1f5ff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Three Iterations of Augmenting Path Discovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[Source s]
    nodeU1[Node A]
    nodeU2[Node B]
    nodeT[Sink t]

    nodeS -- Capacity 16 --> nodeU1
    nodeS -- Capacity 13 --> nodeU2
    nodeU1 -- Capacity 10 --> nodeU2
    nodeU1 -- Capacity 12 --> nodeT
    nodeU2 -- Capacity 15 --> nodeT

    style nodeS fill:#ffe082
    style nodeT fill:#ef9a9a
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> The schematic above represents the **physical network topology**. In an actual KTU board examination, students are expected to **draw the network manually** with arc labels for both capacity and flow values, marking each augmenting path with a coloured highlighter or distinct line style to differentiate successive iterations.

### 4.3 Logical Flow: Augmenting Path Classification Table

| **Path Type** | **Arc Direction Along Path** | **Residual Condition** | **Augmentation Operation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Forward Path | All arcs forward | $r_{ij} > 0$ for all arcs | Add $\delta$ to each arc flow |
| Pure Backward Path | All arcs backward | $f_{ji} > 0$ for all arcs | Subtract $\delta$ from each arc flow |
| Mixed Path | Combination of forward and backward | $r_{ij} > 0$ forward, $f_{ji} > 0$ backward | Add/subtract $\delta$ based on direction |
| Saturated Path (Invalid) | At least one saturated arc | $r_{ij} = 0$ for some arc | Not an augmenting path |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> When presenting an augmenting path solution in the answer sheet, always explicitly mark the **direction of traversal** with arrows and clearly distinguish between **forward arcs** (saturated to capacity) and **backward arcs** (used to cancel prior flow). Examiners reward this clarity with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Conceptual Recall)

#### **Question 1: Define Augmenting Path**
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**
An **augmenting path** in a flow network is a path from the source node $s$ to the sink node $t$ in the residual network $G(f)$ along which it is possible to push additional positive flow. For every arc $(i,j)$ traversed in its forward direction, the residual capacity $r_{ij} = c_{ij} - f_{ij} > 0$.
